title: Reality television

infobox.value: is a [genre](/wiki/Genre "Genre") of [television programming](/wiki/Television_program "Television program") that documents purportedly unscripted real-life situations, often starring ordinary people rather than professional actors. Reality television emerged as a distinct genre in the early 1990s with shows such as *[The Real World](/wiki/The_Real_World "The Real World")*, then achieved prominence in the early 2000s with the success of the series *[Survivor](/wiki/Survivor_(franchise) "Survivor (franchise)")*, *[Idol](/wiki/Idol_(franchise) "Idol (franchise)")*, and *[Big Brother](/wiki/Big_Brother_(franchise) "Big Brother (franchise)")*, all of which became global [franchises.](/wiki/Franchising "Franchising")[[1]](#cite_note-hill05-1) Reality television shows tend to be interspersed with "confessionals", short interview segments in which cast members reflect on or provide context for the events being depicted on-screen; this is most commonly seen in American reality television. Competition-based reality shows typically feature the gradual elimination of participants, either by a panel of judges, by the viewership of the show, or by the contestants themselves.

From Wikipedia, the free encyclopedia

Genre of television programming

"Reality TV" and "Reality Show" redirect here. For the channel formerly known as "Reality TV", see [CBS Reality](/wiki/CBS_Reality "CBS Reality"). For other uses of "Reality Show", see [Reality Show (disambiguation)](/wiki/Reality_Show_(disambiguation) "Reality Show (disambiguation)").

**Reality television** is a [genre](/wiki/Genre "Genre") of [television programming](/wiki/Television_program "Television program") that documents purportedly unscripted real-life situations, often starring ordinary people rather than professional actors. Reality television emerged as a distinct genre in the early 1990s with shows such as *[The Real World](/wiki/The_Real_World "The Real World")*, then achieved prominence in the early 2000s with the success of the series *[Survivor](/wiki/Survivor_(franchise) "Survivor (franchise)")*, *[Idol](/wiki/Idol_(franchise) "Idol (franchise)")*, and *[Big Brother](/wiki/Big_Brother_(franchise) "Big Brother (franchise)")*, all of which became global [franchises.](/wiki/Franchising "Franchising")[[1]](#cite_note-hill05-1) Reality television shows tend to be interspersed with "confessionals", short interview segments in which cast members reflect on or provide context for the events being depicted on-screen; this is most commonly seen in American reality television. Competition-based reality shows typically feature the gradual elimination of participants, either by a panel of judges, by the viewership of the show, or by the contestants themselves.

[Documentaries](/wiki/Documentary_film "Documentary film"), [factual television](/wiki/Factual_television "Factual television") - such as shows based around the exploration of science and technology - [television news](/wiki/Television_news "Television news"), [sports television](/wiki/Sports_television "Sports television"), [talk shows](/wiki/Talk_show "Talk show"), and traditional [game shows](/wiki/Game_show "Game show") are generally not classified as reality television. Some genres of television programming that predate the reality television boom have been retroactively classified as reality television, including [hidden camera](/wiki/Hidden_camera "Hidden camera") shows, talent-search shows, documentary series about ordinary people, high-concept game shows, home improvement shows, and [court shows](/wiki/Court_show "Court show") featuring real-life cases and issues.[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*]

Reality television has [faced significant criticism](/wiki/Criticism_of_reality_television "Criticism of reality television") since its rise in popularity. Critics argue that by placing the participants in artificial situations, coaching them on behavior, generating storylines ahead of time, staging the scenes and editing footage in misleading ways, reality television shows do not accurately reflect reality. Some shows have been accused of rigging the favorite or underdog to win. Other criticisms of reality television shows include that they humiliate or exploit participants; that they make stars out of either untalented people or infamous figures or both; and that they glamorize vulgarity. This has been a recurring pattern in the reality TV world, especially when it comes to normalizing cruelty and violence. Reality TV has been known to favor disruptive behavior because it enhances the drama and entertainment factor of the show. This pattern often leaves cast members with many mental health issues, and personal self-esteem problems. [*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*]

## History

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Reality_television&action=edit&section=1 "Edit section: History")]

Television formats portraying ordinary people in unscripted situations are almost as old as the television medium itself. Producer-host [Allen Funt](/wiki/Allen_Funt "Allen Funt")'s *[Candid Camera](/wiki/Candid_Camera "Candid Camera")*, in which unsuspecting people were confronted with funny, unusual situations and filmed with hidden cameras, first aired in 1948. In the 21st century, the series is often considered a prototype of reality television programming.[[2]](#cite_note-2)[[3]](#cite_note-3)

### 1940s–1950s

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Reality_television&action=edit&section=2 "Edit section: 1940s–1950s")]

In the early 1940s the German television station [Fernsehsender Paul Nipkow](/wiki/Fernsehsender_Paul_Nipkow "Fernsehsender Paul Nipkow") had staged a show in which a young couple acted as model Aryans and presented their everyday lives without a script to the camera
(*Familienchroniken – Ein Abend mit Hans und Gelli*). Even though it was clearly [Nazi](/wiki/Nazi_Germany "Nazi Germany") propaganda and the episodes were certainly affected by [censorship](/wiki/Censorship "Censorship"), in recent years the show has been presented more frequently as the oldest reality TV show in the world.[[4]](#cite_note-4)

Precedents for television that portrayed people in unscripted situations began in the late 1940s. *[Queen for a Day](/wiki/Queen_for_a_Day "Queen for a Day")* (1945–1964) was an early example of reality-based television. The 1946 television game show *[Cash and Carry](/wiki/Cash_and_Carry_(TV_series) "Cash and Carry (TV series)")* sometimes featured contestants performing stunts. Debuting in 1948, [Allen Funt](/wiki/Allen_Funt "Allen Funt")'s [hidden camera](/wiki/Hidden_camera "Hidden camera") show *[Candid Camera](/wiki/Candid_Camera "Candid Camera")* (based on his previous 1947 radio show, *[The Candid Microphone](/wiki/The_Candid_Microphone "The Candid Microphone")*) broadcast unsuspecting ordinary people reacting to pranks.[[5]](#cite_note-5) In 1948, talent search shows, such as *[Ted Mack's](/wiki/Ted_Mack_(radio-TV_host) "Ted Mack (radio-TV host)") [Original Amateur Hour](/wiki/Original_Amateur_Hour "Original Amateur Hour")* and *[Arthur Godfrey's Talent Scouts](/wiki/Arthur_Godfrey%27s_Talent_Scouts "Arthur Godfrey's Talent Scouts")*, featured amateur competitors and audience voting. In the 1950s, game shows *[Beat the Clock](/wiki/Beat_the_Clock "Beat the Clock")* and *[Truth or Consequences](/wiki/Truth_or_Consequences "Truth or Consequences")* involved contestants in wacky competitions, stunts, and practical jokes. *[Confession](/wiki/Confession_(American_TV_series) "Confession (American TV series)")* was a crime and police show that aired from June 1958 to January 1959, with interviewer Jack Wyatt questioning criminals from assorted backgrounds.[[6]](#cite_note-6) The radio series *Nightwatch* (1951–1955) tape-recorded the daily activities of [Culver City, California](/wiki/Culver_City,_California "Culver City, California") police officers. The series *[You Asked for It](/wiki/You_Asked_for_It "You Asked for It")* (1950–1959) incorporated audience involvement by basing episodes around requests sent in by postcard from viewers.

### 1960s–1970s

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Reality_television&action=edit&section=3 "Edit section: 1960s–1970s")]

First broadcast in the [United Kingdom](/wiki/United_Kingdom "United Kingdom") in 1964, the [Granada Television](/wiki/Granada_Television "Granada Television") documentary *[Seven Up!](/wiki/Up_Series "Up Series")* broadcast interviews with 14 ordinary 7-year-olds from a broad cross-section of society and inquired about their reactions to everyday life. Every seven years, the filmmaker [Michael Apted](/wiki/Michael_Apted "Michael Apted") created a new film documenting the lives of the same individuals during the intervening period. Titled the [Up Series](/wiki/Up_Series "Up Series"), episodes included "7 Plus Seven", "21 Up", etc.; it is still ongoing. Structured as a series of candid interviews with no scripted plot, the films chronicle how the participants navigated personal milestones such as education, career, marriage, and family. Over time, by virtue of the sustained public attention, the participants became notable figures in British culture, effectively turning ordinary people into a type of celebrity.[[7]](#cite_note-7)

The series *[The American Sportsman](/wiki/The_American_Sportsman "The American Sportsman")*, which ran from 1965 to 1986 on [ABC](/wiki/American_Broadcasting_Company "American Broadcasting Company") in the United States,[[8]](#cite_note-8)[[9]](#cite_note-9) would typically feature one or more celebrities, and sometimes their family members, being accompanied by a camera crew on an [outdoor adventure](/wiki/Adventure#Outdoors "Adventure"), such as [hunting](/wiki/Hunting "Hunting"), [fishing](/wiki/Fishing "Fishing"), hiking, [scuba diving](/wiki/Scuba_diving "Scuba diving"), rock climbing, wildlife photography, horseback riding, race car driving, and the like, with most of the resulting action and dialogue being unscripted, except for the narration.

In the 1966 [Direct Cinema](/wiki/Direct_Cinema "Direct Cinema") film *[Chelsea Girls](/wiki/Chelsea_Girls "Chelsea Girls")*, [Andy Warhol](/wiki/Andy_Warhol "Andy Warhol") filmed various acquaintances with no direction given. The *[Radio Times](/wiki/Radio_Times "Radio Times") Guide to Film 2007* said that the film was "to blame for reality television".[[10]](#cite_note-10)

In 1969, the British rock group [the Beatles](/wiki/The_Beatles "The Beatles") were filmed for a month during the recording sessions which would become their album *[Let It Be](/wiki/Let_It_Be_(album) "Let It Be (album)")* and released the [homonymous film](/wiki/Let_It_Be_(1970_film) "Let It Be (1970 film)") the following year. In 2021, director [Peter Jackson](/wiki/Peter_Jackson "Peter Jackson") created an eight-hour, three-episode television series entitled *[The Beatles: Get Back](/wiki/The_Beatles:_Get_Back "The Beatles: Get Back")*.[[11]](#cite_note-11)

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/b/b5/The_Loud_Family_1973.JPG/250px-The_Loud_Family_1973.JPG)](/wiki/File:The_Loud_Family_1973.JPG)

The Loud family, subjects of the pioneering [PBS](/wiki/PBS "PBS") series *[An American Family](/wiki/An_American_Family "An American Family")*. During filming, the parents decided to divorce and son [Lance](/wiki/Lance_Loud "Lance Loud") (top right) came out as gay.

The 12-part 1973 [PBS](/wiki/PBS "PBS") series *[An American Family](/wiki/An_American_Family "An American Family")* showed a [nuclear family](/wiki/Nuclear_family "Nuclear family") (filmed in 1971) going through a divorce; unlike many later reality shows, it was more or less documentary in purpose and style. In 1974 a counterpart program, *[The Family](/wiki/The_Family_(1974_UK_TV_series) "The Family (1974 UK TV series)")*, was made in the UK, following the working-class Wilkins family of [Reading](/wiki/Reading,_Berkshire "Reading, Berkshire").[[12]](#cite_note-Biressi-12) Other forerunners of modern reality television were the 1970s productions of [Chuck Barris](/wiki/Chuck_Barris "Chuck Barris"): *[The Dating Game](/wiki/The_Dating_Game "The Dating Game")*, *[The Newlywed Game](/wiki/The_Newlywed_Game "The Newlywed Game")*, and *[The Gong Show](/wiki/The_Gong_Show "The Gong Show")*, all of which featured participants who were eager to sacrifice some of their privacy and dignity in a televised competition.[[13]](#cite_note-13)

The 1976–1980 [BBC](/wiki/BBC "BBC") series *[The Big Time](/wiki/The_Big_Time_(TV_series) "The Big Time (TV series)")* featured a different amateur in some field (cooking, comedy, football, etc.) trying to succeed professionally in that field, with help from notable experts. The 15-episode series is credited with starting the career of [Sheena Easton](/wiki/Sheena_Easton "Sheena Easton"), who was selected to appear in the episode showing an aspiring pop singer trying to enter the music business.[[14]](#cite_note-14)

In 1978, *[Living in the Past](/wiki/Living_in_the_Past_(TV_series) "Living in the Past (TV series)")* had amateurs participating in a re-enactment of life in an [Iron Age](/wiki/Iron_Age "Iron Age") English village.

### 1980s–1990s

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Reality_television&action=edit&section=4 "Edit section: 1980s–1990s")]

Producer [George Schlatter](/wiki/George_Schlatter "George Schlatter") capitalized on the advent of videotape to create *[Real People](/wiki/Real_People_(TV_program) "Real People (TV program)")*, a surprise hit for NBC, and it ran from 1979 to 1984. The success of *Real People* was quickly copied by the television company ABC with *[That's Incredible](/wiki/That%27s_Incredible "That's Incredible")*, a stunt show produced by [Alan Landsburg](/wiki/Alan_Landsburg "Alan Landsburg") and co-hosted by [Fran Tarkenton](/wiki/Fran_Tarkenton "Fran Tarkenton"); CBS's entry into the genre was *[That's My Line](/wiki/That%27s_My_Line "That's My Line")*, a series hosted by [Bob Barker](/wiki/Bob_Barker "Bob Barker"). The [Canadian](/wiki/Canada "Canada") series *[Thrill of a Lifetime](/wiki/Thrill_of_a_Lifetime_(TV_series) "Thrill of a Lifetime (TV series)")*, a fantasies-fulfilled reality show, originally ran from 1982 to 1988. It was revived from 2001 to 2003. In 1985, underwater cinematographer Al Giddings teamed with former [Miss Universe](/wiki/Miss_Universe "Miss Universe") [Shawn Weatherly](/wiki/Shawn_Weatherly "Shawn Weatherly") on the NBC series *Oceanquest*, which chronicled Weatherly's adventures scuba diving in various exotic locales. Weatherly was nominated for an [Emmy Award](/wiki/Emmy_Award "Emmy Award") for Outstanding Achievement in informational programming.[[15]](#cite_note-15)
*[COPS](/wiki/Cops_(TV_series) "Cops (TV series)")*, which first aired in the spring of 1989 on [Fox](/wiki/Fox_Broadcasting_Company "Fox Broadcasting Company") and was developed due to the need for new programming during the [1988 Writers Guild of America strike](/wiki/1988_Writers_Guild_of_America_strike "1988 Writers Guild of America strike"),[[16]](#cite_note-16) showed police officers on duty apprehending criminals. It introduced the [camcorder](/wiki/Camcorder "Camcorder") look and [cinéma vérité](/wiki/Cin%C3%A9ma_v%C3%A9rit%C3%A9 "Cinéma vérité") feel of much of later reality television. The 1991 [television documentary](/wiki/Television_documentary "Television documentary") on "typical American high schoolers", *[Yearbook](/wiki/Yearbook_(TV_series) "Yearbook (TV series)")*, focused on [seniors](/wiki/Twelfth_grade "Twelfth grade") attending Glenbard West High School, in [Glen Ellyn](/wiki/Glen_Ellyn,_Illinois "Glen Ellyn, Illinois"), Illinois and [broadcast prime-time](/wiki/Prime_time "Prime time") on [Fox](/wiki/Fox_Broadcasting_Company "Fox Broadcasting Company").

The series *[Nummer 28](/wiki/Nummer_28 "Nummer 28")*, which aired on [Dutch](/wiki/Netherlands "Netherlands") television in 1991, originated the concept of putting strangers together in a limited environment for an extended period of time and recording the drama that ensued. *Nummer 28* also pioneered many of the stylistic conventions that have since become standard in reality television shows, including extensive use of soundtrack music and the interspersing of events on screen with after-the-fact "confessionals" recorded by cast members, which serve as narration. *Nummer 28* became the model for many later series of *Big Brother* and its clones, and Peter Weir's full-length film *[The Truman Show](/wiki/The_Truman_Show "The Truman Show")*. One year later, the same concept was used by [MTV](/wiki/MTV "MTV") in its new series *[The Real World](/wiki/The_Real_World "The Real World")*. *Nummer 28* creator Erik Latour has long claimed that *The Real World* was directly inspired by his show.[[17]](#cite_note-werklozen-17) But the producers of *The Real World* have said that their direct inspiration was *An American Family*.[[18]](#cite_note-18) According to television commentator [Charlie Brooker](/wiki/Charlie_Brooker "Charlie Brooker"), this type of reality television was enabled by the advent of computer-based [non-linear editing systems](/wiki/Non-linear_editing_system "Non-linear editing system") for video (such as produced by [Avid Technology](/wiki/Avid_Technology "Avid Technology")) in 1989. These systems made it easy to quickly edit hours of video footage into a usable form, something that had been very difficult to do before (film, which was easy to edit, was too expensive to use in shooting enough hours on a regular basis).[[19]](#cite_note-CharlieBooker-19)

*[Sylvania Waters](/wiki/Sylvania_Waters_(TV_series) "Sylvania Waters (TV series)")* (1992) was an Australian show that depicted a family, similar in concept to *An American Family*.

The 1994–95 [O. J. Simpson murder case](/wiki/O._J._Simpson_murder_case "O. J. Simpson murder case"), during which live network television followed suspect Simpson for 90 minutes being chased by police, has been described as a seminal moment in reality television. Networks interrupted their regular television programming for months for coverage of the trial and related events. Because of Simpson's status as a top athlete and celebrity, the brutal nature of the murders, and issues of race and class in Los Angeles celebrity culture, the sensational case dominated ratings and the public conversation.[[20]](#cite_note-20)[[21]](#cite_note-21)

Many reality television stars of the 2000s and 2010s have direct or indirect connections to people involved in the case, most notably [Kim Kardashian](/wiki/Kim_Kardashian "Kim Kardashian"), daughter of defense attorney [Robert Kardashian](/wiki/Robert_Kardashian "Robert Kardashian"), and several of her relatives and associates.[[22]](#cite_note-22)[[23]](#cite_note-23)

The series *[Expedition Robinson](/wiki/Expedition_Robinson "Expedition Robinson")*, created by television producer Charlie Parsons, which first aired in 1997 in [Sweden](/wiki/Sweden "Sweden") (and was later produced in a large number of other countries as *[Survivor](/wiki/Survivor_(franchise) "Survivor (franchise)")*), added to the *Nummer 28*/*Real World* template the idea of competition and elimination. Cast members or contestants battled against each other and were removed from the show until only one winner remained (these shows are now sometimes called elimination shows). *[Changing Rooms](/wiki/Changing_Rooms_(TV_show) "Changing Rooms (TV show)")*, a program that began in the UK in 1996, showed couples redecorating each other's houses, and was the first[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*] reality show with a [self-improvement](/wiki/Self-improvement "Self-improvement") or [makeover](/wiki/Makeover "Makeover") theme. The dating reality show *[Streetmate](/wiki/Streetmate "Streetmate")* premiered in the UK in 1998. Originally created by [Gabe Sachs](/wiki/Gabe_Sachs "Gabe Sachs") as *Street Match*, it was a flop in the United States. But the show was revamped in the UK by [Tiger Aspect Productions](/wiki/Tiger_Aspect_Productions "Tiger Aspect Productions") and became a cult hit. The production team from the original series later created the popular reality shows *[Strictly Come Dancing](/wiki/Strictly_Come_Dancing "Strictly Come Dancing")*, *[Location, Location, Location](/wiki/Location,_Location,_Location "Location, Location, Location")*, and the revamped *[MasterChef](/wiki/MasterChef "MasterChef")*, among others.[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*] The 1980s and 1990s were also a time when [tabloid talk shows](/wiki/Tabloid_talk_show "Tabloid talk show") became more popular. Many of these featured the same types of unusual or dysfunctional guests who would later become popular as cast members of reality shows.[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*]

### 2000s

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Reality_television&action=edit&section=5 "Edit section: 2000s")]

Reality television became globally popular at the turn of the century. [*Survivor/Expedition Robinson*](/wiki/Survivor_(franchise) "Survivor (franchise)") debuted in Sweden in 1997, while [*Big Brother*](/wiki/Big_Brother_(franchise) "Big Brother (franchise)") debuted in the Netherlands in 1999. The [American version](/wiki/Survivor_(American_TV_series) "Survivor (American TV series)") of *Survivor* began airing in 2000 becoming a major success. The *Big Brother* franchise especially became a major success and is credited for "changing" television and celebrity status.[[24]](#cite_note-24) The franchise expanded from the Netherlands to many other countries in the year 2000, and that same year [Endemol](/wiki/Endemol "Endemol") (the Dutch TV company behind *Big Brother*) was purchased by Spanish corporation [Telefónica](/wiki/Telef%C3%B3nica "Telefónica") for what was considered a large sum.[[25]](#cite_note-25)

In the United States, reality television programs suffered a temporary decline in viewership, especially following the [September 11](/wiki/September_11_attacks "September 11 attacks") terrorist attacks, leading some entertainment industry columnists to speculate that the genre was a temporary fad that had run its course.[[26]](#cite_note-26) Reality shows that suffered from low ratings included *[The Amazing Race](/wiki/The_Amazing_Race_(American_TV_series) "The Amazing Race (American TV series)")* (although the show has since recovered and is in its 37th edition), *[Lost](/wiki/Lost_(2001_TV_series) "Lost (2001 TV series)")* (unrelated to the better-known [serial drama of the same name](/wiki/Lost_(TV_series) "Lost (TV series)")) and *[The Mole](/wiki/The_Mole_(American_TV_series) "The Mole (American TV series)")* (which was successful in other countries).[[27]](#cite_note-27) But stronghold show *Survivor* remained resilient, leading the ratings in [2001–02](/wiki/2002_in_television "2002 in television").[[28]](#cite_note-28) On the other hand, *[American Idol](/wiki/American_Idol "American Idol")*, which debuted in 2002, would go on to top the U.S. season-average television ratings in the 2000s. *Idol* has the longest hold on the No. 1 rank in the [American television ratings](/wiki/Nielsen_ratings "Nielsen ratings"), dominating over all other primetime programs and other television series in the overall viewership tallies for eight consecutive years, from the [2003–2004](/wiki/2004_in_television "2004 in television") to the [2010–2011](/wiki/2011_in_American_television "2011 in American television") television seasons.

Another trend was to combine reality TV with a social history angle usually by having contestants taken back to various time periods primarily to see how millennials would cope without modern technology. Examples included *[The 1900 House](/wiki/The_1900_House "The 1900 House")*, *[Bad Lads' Army](/wiki/Bad_Lads%27_Army "Bad Lads' Army")* and *[That'll Teach 'Em](/wiki/That%27ll_Teach_%27Em "That'll Teach 'Em")*. In addition to those was a series consisting of archeologists and historians running a farm though various historical periods, most notably *[Victorian Farm](/wiki/Victorian_Farm "Victorian Farm")*.

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/2/2b/John_de_Mol_jr.jpg/250px-John_de_Mol_jr.jpg)](/wiki/File:John_de_Mol_jr.jpg)

Dutch media tycoon [John de Mol Jr.](/wiki/John_de_Mol_Jr. "John de Mol Jr."), who created the reality television franchises *[Big Brother](/wiki/Big_Brother_(franchise) "Big Brother (franchise)")*, *[Fear Factor](/wiki/Fear_Factor_(franchise) "Fear Factor (franchise)")* and *[The Voice](/wiki/The_Voice_(franchise) "The Voice (franchise)")*, among others

Internationally, a number of shows created in the late 1990s and 2000s have had massive global success. Reality-television franchises created during that time that have had more than 30 international adaptations each include the singing competition franchises *[Idols](/wiki/Idols_(franchise) "Idols (franchise)")*,[[29]](#cite_note-29) *[Star Academy](/wiki/Star_Academy "Star Academy")*[[30]](#cite_note-30) and *[The X Factor](/wiki/The_X_Factor "The X Factor")*, other competition franchises *Survivor/Expedition Robinson*, *Big Brother*, *[The Biggest Loser](/wiki/The_Biggest_Loser "The Biggest Loser")*, *[Come Dine with Me](/wiki/Come_Dine_with_Me "Come Dine with Me")*, *[Got Talent](/wiki/Got_Talent "Got Talent")*, *[Top Model](/wiki/Top_Model "Top Model")*, *[MasterChef](/wiki/MasterChef "MasterChef")*, *[Project Runway](/wiki/Project_Runway_(franchise) "Project Runway (franchise)")* and *[Dancing with the Stars](/wiki/Dancing_with_the_Stars "Dancing with the Stars")*, and the investment franchise *[Dragons' Den](/wiki/Dragons%27_Den "Dragons' Den")*. Several "[reality game shows](/wiki/Reality_game_show "Reality game show")" from the same period have had even greater success, including *[Deal or No Deal](/wiki/Deal_or_No_Deal "Deal or No Deal")*, *[Who Wants to Be a Millionaire?](/wiki/Who_Wants_to_Be_a_Millionaire%3F "Who Wants to Be a Millionaire?")*, and *[Weakest Link](/wiki/The_Weakest_Link_(British_game_show) "The Weakest Link (British game show)")*, with over 50 international adaptions each. (All but four of these franchises, *Top Model*, *Project Runway*, *The Biggest Loser* and *Dragons' Den*, were created by either British producers or the Dutch production company [Endemol](/wiki/Endemol "Endemol"). Although *Dragons' Den* originated in [Japan](/wiki/Japan "Japan"), most of its adaptations are based on the British version.) In India, the competition show *[Indian Idol](/wiki/Indian_Idol "Indian Idol")* was the most popular television program for its first six seasons.[[31]](#cite_note-Reality_TV_and_Participatory_Culture_in_India-31)

During the 2000s, several [cable](/wiki/Cable_television "Cable television") networks, including [Bravo](/wiki/Bravo_(American_TV_network) "Bravo (American TV network)"), [A&E](/wiki/A%26E_(TV_network) "A&E (TV network)"), [E!](/wiki/E! "E!"), [TLC](/wiki/TLC_(TV_network) "TLC (TV network)"), [History](/wiki/History_(American_TV_network) "History (American TV network)"), [VH1](/wiki/VH1 "VH1"), and [MTV](/wiki/MTV "MTV"), changed their programming to feature mostly reality television series.[[32]](#cite_note-simple-econ-32) *[The Osbournes](/wiki/The_Osbournes "The Osbournes")* is considered to be a pioneering reality television show which ushered in an era of series focusing on celebrity family life.[[33]](#cite_note-33)[[34]](#cite_note-34) In addition, three cable channels were started around that time that were devoted exclusively to reality television: [Fox Reality](/wiki/Fox_Reality "Fox Reality") in the United States, which operated from 2005 to 2010; [Global Reality Channel](/wiki/Global_Reality_Channel "Global Reality Channel") in [Canada](/wiki/Canada "Canada"), which lasted two years from 2010 to 2012; and [CBS Reality](/wiki/CBS_Reality "CBS Reality") (formerly known as Reality TV and then Zone Reality) in Europe, the Middle East and Africa, which has run from 1999 to the present.

During the early part of the 2000s, network executives expressed concern that reality-television programming was limited in its appeal for DVD reissue and [syndication](/wiki/Broadcast_syndication "Broadcast syndication"). But DVDs for reality shows sold briskly; *[Laguna Beach: The Real Orange County](/wiki/Laguna_Beach:_The_Real_Orange_County "Laguna Beach: The Real Orange County")*, *[The Amazing Race](/wiki/The_Amazing_Race_(American_TV_series) "The Amazing Race (American TV series)")*, *[Project Runway](/wiki/Project_Runway "Project Runway")*, and *[America's Next Top Model](/wiki/America%27s_Next_Top_Model "America's Next Top Model")* all ranked in the top DVDs sold on [Amazon.com](/wiki/Amazon.com "Amazon.com"). In the mid-2000s, DVDs of *[The Simple Life](/wiki/The_Simple_Life "The Simple Life")* outranked scripted shows such as *[The O.C.](/wiki/The_O.C. "The O.C.")* and *[Desperate Housewives](/wiki/Desperate_Housewives "Desperate Housewives")*. Syndication, however, has been problematic; shows such as *[Fear Factor](/wiki/Fear_Factor "Fear Factor")*, *COPS*, and *[Wife Swap](/wiki/Wife_Swap_(American_TV_series) "Wife Swap (American TV series)")*, in which each episode is self-contained, can be rerun fairly easily, but usually only on cable television or during the daytime (*COPS* and *[America's Funniest Home Videos](/wiki/America%27s_Funniest_Home_Videos "America's Funniest Home Videos")* being exceptions). Season-long competitions, such as *[The Amazing Race](/wiki/The_Amazing_Race_(American_TV_series) "The Amazing Race (American TV series)")*, *[Survivor](/wiki/Survivor_(American_TV_series) "Survivor (American TV series)")*, and *[America's Next Top Model](/wiki/America%27s_Next_Top_Model "America's Next Top Model")* generally perform more poorly and usually must be rerun in [marathons](/wiki/Marathon_(television) "Marathon (television)") to draw the necessary viewers to make it worthwhile. (Even in these cases, it is not always successful: the first ten seasons of *[Dancing with the Stars](/wiki/Dancing_with_the_Stars_(American_TV_series) "Dancing with the Stars (American TV series)")* were picked up by [GSN](/wiki/Game_Show_Network "Game Show Network") in 2012 and was run in marathon format, but attracted low viewership and had very poor ratings). Another option is to create documentaries around series, including extended interviews with the participants and outtakes not seen in the original airings; the syndicated series *[American Idol Rewind](/wiki/American_Idol_Rewind "American Idol Rewind")* is an example of this strategy.

*COPS* has had huge success in syndication, direct response sales, and DVD. A Fox staple since 1989, *COPS* has, as of 2013 (when it moved to cable channel [Spike](/wiki/Spike_(TV_channel) "Spike (TV channel)")), outlasted all competing scripted police shows. Another series that had wide success is *[Cheaters](/wiki/Cheaters_(American_TV_series) "Cheaters (American TV series)")*, which has been running since 2000 in the U.S. and is syndicated in over 100 countries worldwide. In 2001, the [Academy of Television Arts and Sciences](/wiki/Academy_of_Television_Arts_and_Sciences "Academy of Television Arts and Sciences") added the reality genre to the [Emmy Awards](/wiki/Emmy_Awards "Emmy Awards") in the category of [Outstanding Reality Program](/wiki/Primetime_Emmy_Award_for_Outstanding_Reality_Program "Primetime Emmy Award for Outstanding Reality Program"). In 2003, to better differentiate between competition and informational reality programs, a second category, [Outstanding Reality Competition Program](/wiki/Primetime_Emmy_Award_for_Outstanding_Reality_Competition_Program "Primetime Emmy Award for Outstanding Reality Competition Program"), was added. In 2008, a third category, [Outstanding Host for a Reality or Reality-Competition Program](/wiki/Primetime_Emmy_Award_for_Outstanding_Host_for_a_Reality_or_Reality-Competition_Program "Primetime Emmy Award for Outstanding Host for a Reality or Reality-Competition Program"), was added. In 2007, the [web series](/wiki/Web_series "Web series") *[The Next Internet Millionaire](/wiki/The_Next_Internet_Millionaire "The Next Internet Millionaire")* appeared; it was a competition show based in part on *[The Apprentice](/wiki/The_Apprentice_(franchise) "The Apprentice (franchise)")* and was billed as the world's first Internet reality show.

### 2010s

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Reality_television&action=edit&section=6 "Edit section: 2010s")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/1/1a/Duncan_Laurence_with_the_2019_Eurovision_Trophy_%28cropped%29.jpg/250px-Duncan_Laurence_with_the_2019_Eurovision_Trophy_%28cropped%29.jpg)](/wiki/File:Duncan_Laurence_with_the_2019_Eurovision_Trophy_(cropped).jpg)

[Duncan Laurence](/wiki/Duncan_Laurence "Duncan Laurence"), who competed on the [fifth season](/wiki/The_Voice_of_Holland_season_5 "The Voice of Holland season 5") of singing competition show *[The Voice of Holland](/wiki/The_Voice_of_Holland "The Voice of Holland")* in 2014, with the trophy for winning the [Eurovision Song Contest 2019](/wiki/Eurovision_Song_Contest_2019 "Eurovision Song Contest 2019")

In 2010, Dutch network [RTL 4](/wiki/RTL_4 "RTL 4") premiered *[The Voice of Holland](/wiki/The_Voice_of_Holland "The Voice of Holland")*, a new singing competition devised by former Endemol director [John de Mol Jr.](/wiki/John_de_Mol_Jr. "John de Mol Jr."). The format was differentiated primarily by its "blind audition" process, an opening round in which the judges ("coaches") are initially seated with their backs to the performers in rotating chairs, and must appraise them solely by their vocal performances. The judge can then press a button to signify they are interested in adding the singer to their team, which causes the chair to spin around and reveal the performer.[[35]](#cite_note-35)[[36]](#cite_note-Galjaard-36)

*[The Tester](/wiki/The_Tester "The Tester")* (2010–2012)—a reality series following contestants competing for a chance to become a [game tester](/wiki/Game_testing "Game testing") at [Sony Computer Entertainment](/wiki/Sony_Computer_Entertainment "Sony Computer Entertainment"), was the first original live-action television program to be distributed exclusively via [video game consoles](/wiki/Video_game_console "Video game console"), with its episodes being streamed via [PlayStation Network](/wiki/PlayStation_Network "PlayStation Network").[[37]](#cite_note-USAToday-37)

By 2012, many of the long-running reality television show franchises in the United States, such as *American Idol*, *Dancing with the Stars* and *[The Bachelor](/wiki/The_Bachelor_(American_TV_series) "The Bachelor (American TV series)")*, had begun to see declining ratings.[[38]](#cite_note-38) However, reality television as a whole remained durable in the U.S., with hundreds of shows across many channels. In 2012, *[New York](/wiki/New_York_(magazine) "New York (magazine)")* Magazine's *Vulture* blog published a humorous [Venn diagram](/wiki/Venn_diagram "Venn diagram") showing popular themes across American reality shows then running, including shows set in the U.S. states of [Alaska](/wiki/Alaska "Alaska"), [Louisiana](/wiki/Louisiana "Louisiana") and [Texas](/wiki/Texas "Texas"), shows about cakes, weddings and [pawnbrokers](/wiki/Pawnbroker "Pawnbroker"), and shows, usually competition-based, whose title includes the word "Wars".[[39]](#cite_note-39)

*[Duck Dynasty](/wiki/Duck_Dynasty "Duck Dynasty")* (2012–2017), which focused on the Robertson family that founded [Duck Commander](/wiki/Duck_Commander "Duck Commander"), in 2013 became the most popular reality series in American cable television history. Its fourth-season premiere was viewed by nearly 12 million viewers in the United States, most of which were in rural markets. Its rural audience share ranked in the 30s, an extremely high number for any series, broadcast or cable.

Following from the *1900 House* format, the BBC produced a series called *[Back in Time for Tea](/wiki/Back_in_Time_for_Tea "Back in Time for Tea")* in which a family would experience tea time for various decades.

In 2014, *[Entertainment Weekly](/wiki/Entertainment_Weekly "Entertainment Weekly")* and *[Variety](/wiki/Variety_(magazine) "Variety (magazine)")* again noted a stagnation in reality television programs' ratings in the U.S., which they attributed to "The diminishing returns of cable TV's sea of reality sameness". They noted that a number of networks that featured reality programming, including Bravo and E!, were launching their first scripted shows, and others, including [AMC](/wiki/AMC_(TV_channel) "AMC (TV channel)"), were abandoning plans to launch further reality programs; though they clarified that the genre as a whole "isn't going anywhere."[[40]](#cite_note-40)[[41]](#cite_note-41) Ratings and profits from reality TV continued to decline in the late 2010s.[[42]](#cite_note-42)

In the mid-2010s, South Korean variety formats began to see increased international attention within the reality television market; the reality travelogue *Grandpas Over Flowers* was adapted by American network [NBC](/wiki/NBC "NBC") as *[Better Late Than Never](/wiki/Better_Late_Than_Never_(TV_series) "Better Late Than Never (TV series)")*, becoming the first Korean variety format to ever be adapted by an American broadcaster.*[[43]](#cite_note-THR2-43)[[44]](#cite_note-variety1-44)[[45]](#cite_note-45)* The Korean music competition series *[King of Mask Singer](/wiki/King_of_Mask_Singer "King of Mask Singer")* (2015)—which features celebrity contestants concealed by elaborate [costume masks](/wiki/Mask "Mask"), and only revealing their identity once eliminated—was initially imported to other Asian territories such as [Thailand](/wiki/Thailand "Thailand"). Its [American adaptation](/wiki/The_Masked_Singer_(American_TV_series) "The Masked Singer (American TV series)") by Fox was the third highest-rated series overall of both the 2018–19 and 2019–20 television seasons.[[46]](#cite_note-46)[[47]](#cite_note-47)[[48]](#cite_note-RS_Jun_6-48)[[49]](#cite_note-49)

### 2020s

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Reality_television&action=edit&section=7 "Edit section: 2020s")]

Television development across all genres was impacted in 2020 by the [COVID-19 pandemic](/wiki/COVID-19_pandemic "COVID-19 pandemic"), which forced many reality competition series to suspend production (and in some cases curtail a competition already in progress, such as [Canadian](/wiki/Big_Brother_Canada_season_8 "Big Brother Canada season 8") and [Malayalam](/wiki/Bigg_Boss_(Malayalam_season_2) "Bigg Boss (Malayalam season 2)") versions of *Big Brother*),[[50]](#cite_note-charity-50)[[51]](#cite_note-51)[[52]](#cite_note-52) until such time that production could recommence with appropriate health and safety protocols approved by local authorities.[[53]](#cite_note-53)[[54]](#cite_note-54) Due to their quicker turnaround times, the American networks used reality series and other unscripted content (including those delayed from their summer lineups) to fill gaps in their schedules while the production of scripted programming resumed.[[55]](#cite_note-:23-55)[[56]](#cite_note-:25-56)

The popularity of *The Masked Singer* led to Fox greenlighting other guesswork-based game and reality shows in the early-2020s, such as the spinoff *[The Masked Dancer](/wiki/The_Masked_Dancer_(American_TV_series) "The Masked Dancer (American TV series)")*, fellow South Korean series *[I Can See Your Voice](/wiki/I_Can_See_Your_Voice_(American_game_show) "I Can See Your Voice (American game show)")* (where participants guess who among a group of contestants are good singers without ever hearing them sing), *[Game of Talents](/wiki/Game_of_Talents "Game of Talents")* (where contestants guessed the talent of a variety act via clues),*[[57]](#cite_note-57)*[[58]](#cite_note-58) *[We Are Family](/wiki/We_Are_Family_(TV_series) "We Are Family (TV series)")* (where contestants identified performers who are relatives of celebrities),[[59]](#cite_note-59) and *[Crime Scene Kitchen](/wiki/Crime_Scene_Kitchen "Crime Scene Kitchen")* (where contestants must identify the [dessert](/wiki/Dessert "Dessert") that was prepared in a kitchen based on clues, and then attempt to recreate the dish themselves).[[60]](#cite_note-60)[[61]](#cite_note-61)

At the same time, reality television continued to evolve in response to broader platform shifts and changing audience behaviors. The rise of streaming services such as Netflix, Amazon Prime Video, and HBO Max led to a new wave of reality formats, including dating shows like *[Love Is Blind](/wiki/Love_Is_Blind_(TV_series) "Love Is Blind (TV series)")* (2020–present) and *[Too Hot to Handle](/wiki/Too_Hot_to_Handle_(TV_series) "Too Hot to Handle (TV series)")* (2020–present), competitive series like *[The Circle](/wiki/The_Circle_(American_TV_series) "The Circle (American TV series)")* (2020–present), and docu-soaps such as *[Dubai Bling](/wiki/Dubai_Bling "Dubai Bling")* (2022–present).[[62]](#cite_note-62)[[63]](#cite_note-63) Reality competitions centered around specialized skills, such as *[Is It Cake?](/wiki/Is_It_Cake%3F "Is It Cake?")* (2022–present), gained popularity for offering cozy, niche viewing experiences that appealed to audiences seeking lighter, feel-good content.[[64]](#cite_note-64) The demand for escapism and creativity during a period of global uncertainty led networks and streaming platforms alike to invest in more experimental and genre-blending formats.[[65]](#cite_note-65)

Internationally, there was also a surge in non-Western reality content gaining global traction, particularly from South Korea, Japan, and India. Shows like *[Single's Inferno](/wiki/Single%27s_Inferno "Single's Inferno")* (South Korea, 2021–present), *[Physical: 100](/wiki/Physical:_100 "Physical: 100")* (South Korea, 2023–present), *[Indian Matchmaking](/wiki/Indian_Matchmaking "Indian Matchmaking")* (India/USA, 2020–present showcased a blend of competition, romance, and cultural elements that resonated with worldwide audiences through platforms like Netflix.[[66]](#cite_note-66)[[67]](#cite_note-67)[[68]](#cite_note-68)

## Subgenres

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Reality_television&action=edit&section=8 "Edit section: Subgenres")]

There have been various attempts to classify reality television shows into different subgenres:

* A 2006 study proposed six subgenres: romance, crime, informational, reality-drama, competition or game, and talent.[[69]](#cite_note-69)
* A 2007 study proposed five subgenres: infotainment, docusoap, lifestyle, reality game shows, and lifestyle experiment programs.[[70]](#cite_note-70)
* A 2009 study proposed eight subgenres: "gamedocs", dating programs, makeover programs, docusoaps, talent contests, court programs, reality sitcoms, and celebrity variations of other programs.[[71]](#cite_note-71)

Another categorization divides reality television into two types: shows that purport to document real life, and shows that place participants in new circumstances. In a 2003 paper, theorists Elisabeth Klaus and Stephanie Lücke referred to the former category as "docusoaps", which consist of "narrative reality", and the latter category as "reality soaps", which consist of "performative reality".[[72]](#cite_note-72) Since 2014, the [Primetime Emmy Awards](/wiki/Primetime_Emmy_Awards "Primetime Emmy Awards") have used a similar classification, with separate awards for "[unstructured reality](/wiki/Primetime_Emmy_Award_for_Outstanding_Unstructured_Reality_Program "Primetime Emmy Award for Outstanding Unstructured Reality Program")" and "[structured reality](/wiki/Primetime_Emmy_Award_for_Outstanding_Structured_Reality_Program "Primetime Emmy Award for Outstanding Structured Reality Program")" programs, as well as a third award for "[reality-competition](/wiki/Primetime_Emmy_Award_for_Outstanding_Reality-Competition_Program "Primetime Emmy Award for Outstanding Reality-Competition Program")" programs.

### Documentary-style

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Reality_television&action=edit&section=9 "Edit section: Documentary-style")]

In many reality television programs, camera shooting and footage editing give the viewer the impression that they are passive observers following people going about their daily personal and professional activities; this style of filming is sometimes referred to as [fly on the wall](/wiki/Fly_on_the_wall "Fly on the wall"), [observational documentary](/wiki/Observational_documentary "Observational documentary") or [factual television](/wiki/Factual_television "Factual television"). Story "plots" are often constructed via editing or planned situations, with the results resembling [soap operas](/wiki/Soap_opera "Soap opera") – hence the terms *docusoap* and *[docudrama](/wiki/Docudrama "Docudrama")*. [Documentary-style programs](/wiki/Television_documentary "Television documentary") give viewers a private look into the lives of the subjects.

Within documentary-style reality television are several subcategories or variants:

#### First responder reality style

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Reality_television&action=edit&section=10 "Edit section: First responder reality style")]

Please see list of programs: [First responder reality shows](/wiki/List_of_reality_television_programs#First_responder_shows "List of reality television programs")

#### Food reality television

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Reality_television&action=edit&section=11 "Edit section: Food reality television")]

Please see main article: [Food reality television](/wiki/Food_reality_television "Food reality television")

#### Soap-opera style

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Reality_television&action=edit&section=12 "Edit section: Soap-opera style")]

Although the term "docusoap" has been used for many documentary-style reality television shows, there have been shows that have deliberately tried to mimic the appearance and structure of soap operas. Such shows often focus on a close-knit group of people and their shifting friendships and romantic relationships. One highly influential such series was the American 2004–2006 series *[Laguna Beach: The Real Orange County](/wiki/Laguna_Beach:_The_Real_Orange_County "Laguna Beach: The Real Orange County"),* which attempted to specifically mimic the primetime soap opera *[The O.C.](/wiki/The_O.C. "The O.C.")*, which had begun airing in 2003. *Laguna Beach* had a more drama-like feel than any previous reality television show, through the use of higher-quality lighting and cameras, voice-over narration instead of on-screen "confessionals", and slower pacing.[[73]](#cite_note-73) *Laguna Beach* led to several spinoff series, most notably the 2006–2010 series *[The Hills](/wiki/The_Hills_(TV_series) "The Hills (TV series)")*. It also inspired various other series, including the highly successful British series *[The Only Way Is Essex](/wiki/The_Only_Way_Is_Essex "The Only Way Is Essex")* and *[Made in Chelsea](/wiki/Made_in_Chelsea "Made in Chelsea")*, and the Australian series *[Freshwater Blue](/wiki/Freshwater_Blue "Freshwater Blue")*.

Due to their dramatized feel, many of these shows have been accused of being pre-scripted, more so than other reality television shows have. The producers of *The Only Way Is Essex* and *Made in Chelsea* have admitted to coaching cast members on what to say in order to draw more emotion from each scene, although they insist that the underlying stories are real.[[74]](#cite_note-74)

Another highly successful group of soap-opera-style shows is the *[Real Housewives](/wiki/The_Real_Housewives "The Real Housewives")* franchise, which began with *[The Real Housewives of Orange County](/wiki/The_Real_Housewives_of_Orange_County "The Real Housewives of Orange County")* in 2006 and has since spawned nearly twenty other series, in the U.S. and internationally. The franchise has an older cast and different personal dynamics than that of *Laguna Beach* and its imitators, as well as lower production values, but similarly is meant to resemble scripted soap operas – in this case, the television series *[Desperate Housewives](/wiki/Desperate_Housewives "Desperate Housewives")* and *[Peyton Place](/wiki/Peyton_Place_(TV_series) "Peyton Place (TV series)")*.

A notable subset of such series focus on a group of women who are romantically connected to male celebrities; these include *[Basketball Wives](/wiki/Basketball_Wives "Basketball Wives")* (2010), *[Love & Hip Hop](/wiki/Love_%26_Hip_Hop "Love & Hip Hop")* (2011), *[Hollywood Exes](/wiki/Hollywood_Exes "Hollywood Exes")* (2012), *Ex-Wives of Rock* (2012) and *[WAGS](/wiki/WAGS_(TV_series) "WAGS (TV series)")* (2015). Most of these shows have had spin-offs in multiple locations.

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/d/d5/Kim_Kardashian_3.JPG/250px-Kim_Kardashian_3.JPG)](/wiki/File:Kim_Kardashian_3.JPG)

Reality TV personality [Kim Kardashian](/wiki/Kim_Kardashian "Kim Kardashian")

There are also fly-on-the-wall-style shows directly involving celebrities. Often these show a celebrity going about their everyday life: notable examples include *[The Anna Nicole Show](/wiki/The_Anna_Nicole_Show "The Anna Nicole Show")*, *[The Osbournes](/wiki/The_Osbournes "The Osbournes")*, *[Gene Simmons Family Jewels](/wiki/Gene_Simmons_Family_Jewels "Gene Simmons Family Jewels")*, *[Newlyweds: Nick and Jessica](/wiki/Newlyweds:_Nick_and_Jessica "Newlyweds: Nick and Jessica")*, *[Keeping Up with the Kardashians](/wiki/Keeping_Up_with_the_Kardashians "Keeping Up with the Kardashians")* and *[Hogan Knows Best](/wiki/Hogan_Knows_Best "Hogan Knows Best")*. [VH1](/wiki/VH1 "VH1") in the mid-2000s had an entire block of such shows, known as "Celebreality". Shows such as these are often created with the idea of promoting a celebrity product or upcoming project.

#### Subcultures

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Reality_television&action=edit&section=13 "Edit section: Subcultures")]

Some documentary-style shows shed light on rarely seen cultures and lifestyles. One example is shows about [people with disabilities](/wiki/People_with_disabilities "People with disabilities")[[75]](#cite_note-75) or people who have unusual physical circumstances, such as the American series *[Push Girls](/wiki/Push_Girls "Push Girls")* and *[Little People, Big World](/wiki/Little_People,_Big_World "Little People, Big World")*, and the British programmes *[Beyond Boundaries](/wiki/Beyond_Boundaries "Beyond Boundaries")*, *[Britain's Missing Top Model](/wiki/Britain%27s_Missing_Top_Model "Britain's Missing Top Model")*, *[The Undateables](/wiki/The_Undateables "The Undateables")* and *[Seven Dwarves](/wiki/Seven_Dwarves_(TV_series) "Seven Dwarves (TV series)")*.

Another example is shows that portray the lives of ethnic or religious minorities. Examples include *[All-American Muslim](/wiki/All-American_Muslim "All-American Muslim")* ([Lebanese-American](/wiki/Lebanese-American "Lebanese-American") [Muslims](/wiki/Muslim "Muslim")), *[Shahs of Sunset](/wiki/Shahs_of_Sunset "Shahs of Sunset")* (affluent [Persian-Americans](/wiki/Persian-Americans "Persian-Americans")), *[Sister Wives](/wiki/Sister_Wives "Sister Wives")* (polygamists from a [Mormon](/wiki/Mormon "Mormon") splinter group), *[Breaking Amish](/wiki/Breaking_Amish "Breaking Amish")* and *[Amish Mafia](/wiki/Amish_Mafia "Amish Mafia")* (the [Amish](/wiki/Amish "Amish")), and *[Big Fat Gypsy Weddings](/wiki/Big_Fat_Gypsy_Weddings "Big Fat Gypsy Weddings")* and its spinoffs ([Romani people](/wiki/Romani_people "Romani people")).

The *Real Housewives* franchise offers a window into the lives of social-striving urban and suburban housewives. Many shows focus on wealth and [conspicuous consumption](/wiki/Conspicuous_consumption "Conspicuous consumption"), including *[Platinum Weddings](/wiki/Platinum_Weddings "Platinum Weddings")*, and *[My Super Sweet 16](/wiki/My_Super_Sweet_16 "My Super Sweet 16")*, which documented huge [coming of age](/wiki/Coming_of_age "Coming of age") celebrations thrown by wealthy parents. Conversely, the highly successful *[Here Comes Honey Boo Boo](/wiki/Here_Comes_Honey_Boo_Boo "Here Comes Honey Boo Boo")* and *[Duck Dynasty](/wiki/Duck_Dynasty "Duck Dynasty")* are set in poorer rural areas of the [Southern United States](/wiki/Southern_United_States "Southern United States").

#### Professional activities

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Reality_television&action=edit&section=14 "Edit section: Professional activities")]

Some documentary-style shows portray professionals either going about day-to-day business or performing an entire project over the course of a series. One early example (and the longest running reality show of any genre) is *[Cops](/wiki/Cops_(TV_series) "Cops (TV series)")*,[[76]](#cite_note-76) which debuted in 1989. Other such shows specifically relating to law enforcement include *[The First 48](/wiki/The_First_48 "The First 48")*, *[Dog the Bounty Hunter](/wiki/Dog_the_Bounty_Hunter "Dog the Bounty Hunter")*, *[Police Stop!](/wiki/Police_Stop! "Police Stop!")*, *[Traffic Cops](/wiki/Traffic_Cops "Traffic Cops")*, *[Border Security](/wiki/Border_Security:_Australia%27s_Front_Line "Border Security: Australia's Front Line")* and *[Motorway Patrol](/wiki/Motorway_Patrol "Motorway Patrol")*.[[77]](#cite_note-77)

Shows set at a specific place of business include *[American Chopper](/wiki/American_Chopper "American Chopper")*, *[Miami Ink](/wiki/Miami_Ink "Miami Ink")* and its spinoffs, *[Bikini Barbershop](/wiki/Bikini_Barbershop "Bikini Barbershop")* and *[Lizard Lick Towing](/wiki/Lizard_Lick_Towing "Lizard Lick Towing")*.

Shows that show people working in the same non-business location include *[Airport](/wiki/Airport_(TV_series) "Airport (TV series)")* and *[Bondi Rescue](/wiki/Bondi_Rescue "Bondi Rescue")*.

Shows that portray a set of people in the same line of work, occasionally competing with each other, include *[Deadliest Catch](/wiki/Deadliest_Catch "Deadliest Catch")*, *[Ice Road Truckers](/wiki/Ice_Road_Truckers "Ice Road Truckers")* and *[Million Dollar Listing Los Angeles](/wiki/Million_Dollar_Listing_Los_Angeles "Million Dollar Listing Los Angeles")* and its spinoffs.

#### Financial transactions and appraisals

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Reality_television&action=edit&section=15 "Edit section: Financial transactions and appraisals")]

One notable subset of shows about professional activities is those in which the professionals haggle and engage in financial transactions, often over unique or rare items whose value must first be appraised. Two such shows, both of which have led to multiple spinoff shows, are *[Pawn Stars](/wiki/Pawn_Stars "Pawn Stars")* (about [pawn shops](/wiki/Pawn_shop "Pawn shop")) and *[American Pickers](/wiki/American_Pickers "American Pickers")*. Other shows, while based around such financial transactions, also show elements of its main cast members' personal and professional lives; these shows include *[Hardcore Pawn](/wiki/Hardcore_Pawn "Hardcore Pawn")* and *[Comic Book Men](/wiki/Comic_Book_Men "Comic Book Men")*. Such shows have some antecedent in the British series *[Antiques Roadshow](/wiki/Antiques_Roadshow "Antiques Roadshow")*,[[78]](#cite_note-78) which began airing in 1979 and has since spawned numerous international versions, although that show includes only appraisals and does not include bargaining or other dramatic elements.

#### Special living environment

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Reality_television&action=edit&section=16 "Edit section: Special living environment")]

Some documentary-style programs place cast members, who in most cases previously did not know each other, in staged living environments; *[The Real World](/wiki/The_Real_World "The Real World")* was the originator of this format. In almost every other such type of programming, cast members are given specific challenges or obstacles to overcome. *[Road Rules](/wiki/Road_Rules "Road Rules")*, which first aired in 1995 as a spin-off of *The Real World*, created a show structure where the cast would travel to various countries performing challenges for prizes.[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*]

*[Big Brother](/wiki/Big_Brother_(franchise) "Big Brother (franchise)")* is probably the best-known program of this type in the world, with around 50 international versions having been produced. Other shows in this category, such as *[The 1900 House](/wiki/The_1900_House "The 1900 House")* and *[Lads' Army](/wiki/Lads%27_Army "Lads' Army")*, involve [historical re-enactment](/wiki/Historical_re-enactment "Historical re-enactment"), with cast members living and working as people of a specific time and place. 2001's *[Temptation Island](/wiki/Temptation_Island_(TV_series) "Temptation Island (TV series)")* achieved some notoriety by placing several couples on an island surrounded by single people in order to test the couples' commitment to each other. *[The Challenge](/wiki/The_Challenge_(TV_series) "The Challenge (TV series)")* has contestants living together in an overseas residence, and has been around for over 30 seasons. The format of each season changes, however the main premise of the series involves a daily challenge, nomination process and elimination round. *[U8TV: The Lofters](/wiki/U8TV:_The_Lofters "U8TV: The Lofters")* combined the "special living environment" format with the "professional activity" format noted earlier; in addition to living together in a [loft](/wiki/Loft "Loft"), each member of the show's cast was hired to host a television program for a Canadian cable channel.

*[The Simple Life](/wiki/The_Simple_Life "The Simple Life")*, *[Tommy Lee Goes to College](/wiki/Tommy_Lee_Goes_to_College "Tommy Lee Goes to College")* and *[The Surreal Life](/wiki/The_Surreal_Life "The Surreal Life")* are all shows in which celebrities are put into an unnatural environment.

#### Court shows

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Reality_television&action=edit&section=17 "Edit section: Court shows")]

Main article: [Court show](/wiki/Court_show "Court show")

Originally, court shows were all dramatized and staged programs, with actors playing the litigants, witnesses and lawyers. The cases were either reenactments of real-life cases or cases that were fictionalized altogether. Among examples of staged courtroom dramas are *[Famous Jury Trials](/wiki/Famous_Jury_Trials "Famous Jury Trials")*, *[Your Witness](/wiki/Your_Witness_(TV_series) "Your Witness (TV series)")*, and the first two eras of *[Divorce Court](/wiki/Divorce_Court "Divorce Court")*. *[The People's Court](/wiki/The_People%27s_Court "The People's Court")* revolutionized the genre by introducing the [arbitration-based "reality"](/wiki/Court_show#Arbitration-based_reality_court_show "Court show") format in 1981, later adopted by the vast majority of court shows. The genre experienced a lull in programming after *The People's Court* was canceled in 1993, but then soared after the emergence of *[Judge Judy](/wiki/Judge_Judy "Judge Judy")* in 1996. This led to a slew of other reality court shows, such as *[Judge Mathis](/wiki/Judge_Mathis "Judge Mathis")*, *[Judge Joe Brown](/wiki/Judge_Joe_Brown "Judge Joe Brown")*, *[Judge Alex](/wiki/Judge_Alex "Judge Alex")*, *[Judge Mills Lane](/wiki/Judge_Mills_Lane "Judge Mills Lane")* and *[Judge Hatchett](/wiki/Judge_Hatchett "Judge Hatchett")*.

Though the litigants are legitimate, the "judges" in such shows are actually arbitrators, as these pseudo-judges are not actually presiding in a [court of law](/wiki/Court_of_law "Court of law"). Typically, however, they are retired judges or at least individuals who have had some legal experience.

Courtroom programs are typically [daytime television](/wiki/Daytime_television "Daytime television") shows that air on weekdays.

#### Investments

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Reality_television&action=edit&section=18 "Edit section: Investments")]

The globally syndicated format *[Dragons' Den](/wiki/Dragons%27_Den "Dragons' Den")* shows a group of wealthy investors choosing whether or not to invest in a series of pitched [startup companies](/wiki/Startup_company "Startup company") and [entrepreneurial ventures](/wiki/Entrepreneurship "Entrepreneurship"). The series *[Restaurant Startup](/wiki/Restaurant_Startup "Restaurant Startup")* similarly involves investors, but involves more of a game show element in which restaurant owners compete to prove their worth. The British series *[Show Me the Monet](/wiki/Show_Me_the_Monet_(TV_series) "Show Me the Monet (TV series)")* offers a twist in which artworks' artistic value, rather than their financial value, is appraised by a panel of judges, who determine whether each one will be featured at an exhibition.

#### Outdoor survival

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Reality_television&action=edit&section=19 "Edit section: Outdoor survival")]

Another subgenre places people in wild and challenging natural settings. This includes such shows as *[Survivorman](/wiki/Survivorman "Survivorman")*, *[Man vs. Wild](/wiki/Man_vs._Wild "Man vs. Wild")*, *[Marooned with Ed Stafford](/wiki/Marooned_with_Ed_Stafford "Marooned with Ed Stafford")*, *[Naked and Afraid](/wiki/Naked_and_Afraid "Naked and Afraid")* and *[Alaskan Bush People](/wiki/Alaskan_Bush_People "Alaskan Bush People")*. The shows *Survivor* and *[Get Out Alive with Bear Grylls](/wiki/Get_Out_Alive_with_Bear_Grylls "Get Out Alive with Bear Grylls")* combine outdoor survival with a competition format, although in *Survivor* the competition also involves social dynamics.

#### Self-improvement or makeover

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Reality_television&action=edit&section=20 "Edit section: Self-improvement or makeover")]

Some reality television shows cover a person or group of people improving their lives. Sometimes the same group of people are covered over an entire season (as in *[The Swan](/wiki/The_Swan_(TV_series) "The Swan (TV series)")* and *[Celebrity Fit Club](/wiki/Celebrity_Fit_Club_(US_TV_series) "Celebrity Fit Club (US TV series)")*), but usually there is a new target for improvement in each episode. Despite differences in the content, the format is usually the same: first the show introduces the subjects in their current, less-than-ideal environment. Then the subjects meet with a group of experts, who give the subjects instructions on how to improve things; they offer aid and encouragement along the way. Finally, the subjects are placed back in their environment and they, along with their friends and family and the experts, appraise the changes that have occurred. Other self-improvement or makeover shows include *The Biggest Loser*, *[Extreme Weight Loss](/wiki/Extreme_Weight_Loss "Extreme Weight Loss")* and *[Fat March](/wiki/Fat_March "Fat March")* (which cover weight loss), *[Extreme Makeover](/wiki/Extreme_Makeover "Extreme Makeover")* (entire physical appearance), *[Queer Eye](/wiki/Queer_Eye_(2003_TV_series) "Queer Eye (2003 TV series)")*, *[What Not to Wear](/wiki/What_Not_to_Wear_(UK_TV_series) "What Not to Wear (UK TV series)")*, *[How Do I Look?](/wiki/How_Do_I_Look%3F "How Do I Look?")*, *[Trinny & Susannah Undress...](/wiki/Trinny_%26_Susannah_Undress... "Trinny & Susannah Undress...")* and *[Snog Marry Avoid?](/wiki/Snog_Marry_Avoid%3F "Snog Marry Avoid?")* (style and grooming), *[Supernanny](/wiki/Supernanny "Supernanny")* (child-rearing), *[Made](/wiki/Made_(TV_series) "Made (TV series)")* (life transformation), *[Tool Academy](/wiki/Tool_Academy "Tool Academy")* (relationship building) and *[Charm School](/wiki/Charm_School_(TV_series) "Charm School (TV series)")* and *[From G's to Gents](/wiki/From_G%27s_to_Gents "From G's to Gents")* (self-improvement and manners).

The concept of self-improvement was taken to its extreme with the British show *[Life Laundry](/wiki/Life_Laundry "Life Laundry")*, in which people who had become hoarders, even living in squalor, were given professional assistance. The American television series *[Hoarders](/wiki/Hoarders_(TV_series) "Hoarders (TV series)")* and *[Hoarding: Buried Alive](/wiki/Hoarding:_Buried_Alive "Hoarding: Buried Alive")* follow similar premises, presenting interventions in the lives of people who suffer from [compulsive hoarding](/wiki/Compulsive_hoarding "Compulsive hoarding"). The British series *[Sort Your Life Out](/w/index.php?title=Sort_Your_Life_Out&action=edit&redlink=1 "Sort Your Life Out (page does not exist)")*, presented by [Stacey Solomon](/wiki/Stacey_Solomon "Stacey Solomon"), is similar, but it also redesigns the participants' houses.

In one study, participants who admitted to watching more reality television were more likely to proceed with a desired plastic surgery than those who watched less.[[79]](#cite_note-79)

#### Renovation

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Reality_television&action=edit&section=21 "Edit section: Renovation")]

Some shows makeover part or all of a person's living space, workspace, or vehicle. The American series *This Old House*, which debuted in 1979, features the start-to-finish renovation of different houses through a season; media critic [Jeff Jarvis](/wiki/Jeff_Jarvis "Jeff Jarvis") has speculated that it is "the original reality TV show."[[80]](#cite_note-80) The British show *[Changing Rooms](/wiki/Changing_Rooms_(TV_show) "Changing Rooms (TV show)")*, beginning in 1996 (later remade in the U.S. as *[Trading Spaces](/wiki/Trading_Spaces "Trading Spaces")*) was the first such renovation show that added a game show feel with different weekly contestants.[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*]

House renovation shows are a mainstay on the American and Canadian cable channel [HGTV](/wiki/HGTV "HGTV"), whose renovation shows include the successful franchises *[Flip or Flop](/wiki/Flip_or_Flop_(franchise) "Flip or Flop (franchise)")*, *[Love It or List It](/wiki/Love_It_or_List_It_(franchise) "Love It or List It (franchise)")* and *[Property Brothers](/wiki/Property_Brothers_(franchise) "Property Brothers (franchise)")*, as well as shows such as *[Debbie Travis' Facelift](/wiki/Debbie_Travis%27_Facelift "Debbie Travis' Facelift")*, *[Designed to Sell](/wiki/Designed_to_Sell "Designed to Sell")* and *[Holmes on Homes](/wiki/Holmes_on_Homes "Holmes on Homes")*. Non-HGTV shows in this category include *[Extreme Makeover: Home Edition](/wiki/Extreme_Makeover:_Home_Edition "Extreme Makeover: Home Edition")* and *[While You Were Out](/wiki/While_You_Were_Out_(TV_series) "While You Were Out (TV series)")*.

*[Pimp My Ride](/wiki/Pimp_My_Ride "Pimp My Ride")* and *[Overhaulin'](/wiki/Overhaulin%27 "Overhaulin'")* show vehicles being rebuilt in a customized way.

#### Business improvement

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Reality_television&action=edit&section=22 "Edit section: Business improvement")]

In some shows, one or more experts try to improve a failing small business over the course of each episode. Examples that cover many types of business include *[We Mean Business](/wiki/We_Mean_Business_(TV_series) "We Mean Business (TV series)")* and *[The Profit](/wiki/The_Profit_(TV_series) "The Profit (TV series)")*. Shows geared for a specific type of business include *[Ramsay's Kitchen Nightmares](/wiki/Ramsay%27s_Kitchen_Nightmares "Ramsay's Kitchen Nightmares")* and *[Restaurant: Impossible](/wiki/Restaurant:_Impossible "Restaurant: Impossible")* (for restaurants), *[Bar Rescue](/wiki/Bar_Rescue "Bar Rescue")* (for bars) and *[Hotel Hell](/wiki/Hotel_Hell "Hotel Hell")* (for hotels).

#### Social experiment

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Reality_television&action=edit&section=23 "Edit section: Social experiment")]

Another type of reality program is the [social experiment](/wiki/Social_experiment "Social experiment") which produces drama, conflict, and sometimes transformation. British TV series *[Wife Swap](/wiki/Wife_Swap_(UK_TV_series) "Wife Swap (UK TV series)")*, which began in 2003, and had many spinoffs in the UK and other countries, is a notable example. In the show, people with different values agree to live by each other's social rules for a period of time. Other shows in this category include *[Trading Spouses](/wiki/Trading_Spouses "Trading Spouses")*, *[Bad Girls Club](/wiki/Bad_Girls_Club "Bad Girls Club")* and *[Holiday Showdown](/wiki/Holiday_Showdown "Holiday Showdown")*. *[Faking It](/wiki/Faking_It_(UK_TV_series) "Faking It (UK TV series)")* was a series where people had to learn a new skill and pass themselves off as experts. *[Shattered](/wiki/Shattered_(2004_TV_series) "Shattered (2004 TV series)")* was a controversial 2004 UK series in which contestants competed to see how long they could go [without sleep](/wiki/Sleep_deprivation "Sleep deprivation"). *[Solitary](/wiki/Solitary_(TV_series) "Solitary (TV series)")* was a controversial 2006-2010 [Fox Reality](/wiki/Fox_Reality_Channel "Fox Reality Channel") series which isolated contestants for weeks in [solitary confinement](/wiki/Solitary_confinement "Solitary confinement") pods with limited sleep, food and information while competing in elimination challenges which could be ended by pressing a 'quit' button, causing winners to go on for much longer than needed in a blind gamble to win.

The Dutch series *[De Verraders](/wiki/De_Verraders "De Verraders")*, adapted internationally as *The Traitors*, features contestants divided into two factions—the "traitors" and the "faithful"—and competing in challenges to build a cash jackpot awarded in the finale; three contestants designated as "traitors" (who are known to the viewers) have the ability to secretly eliminate ("murder") other contestants each night, while the remaining contestants are tasked with figuring out the identities of the traitors so they can attempt to "banish" them in elimination votes. The jackpot is split among the faithful if they eliminate all of the traitors, but is split among the traitors if they fail.

#### Hidden cameras

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Reality_television&action=edit&section=24 "Edit section: Hidden cameras")]

Another type of reality programming features [hidden cameras](/wiki/Hidden_camera "Hidden camera") rolling when random passers-by encounter a staged situation. *Candid Camera*, which first aired on television in 1948, pioneered the format. Modern variants of this type of production include *[Punk'd](/wiki/Punk%27d "Punk'd")*, *[Trigger Happy TV](/wiki/Trigger_Happy_TV "Trigger Happy TV")*, *[Primetime: What Would You Do?](/wiki/What_Would_You_Do%3F_(2008_TV_program) "What Would You Do? (2008 TV program)")*, *[The Jamie Kennedy Experiment](/wiki/The_Jamie_Kennedy_Experiment "The Jamie Kennedy Experiment")* and *[Just for Laughs Gags](/wiki/Just_for_Laughs_Gags "Just for Laughs Gags")*. The series *[Scare Tactics](/wiki/Scare_Tactics "Scare Tactics")* and *[Room 401](/wiki/Room_401 "Room 401")* are hidden-camera programs in which the goal is to frighten contestants rather than just befuddle or amuse them. The Belgian hidden camera series *[Sorry voor alles](/wiki/Sorry_voor_alles "Sorry voor alles")* subjects a contestant to various staged situations over a month-long period, designed to analyze their personality and how they respond. After the contestant is taken to a studio and let off the hook, they then answer observation questions related to the events for a chance to win prizes.[[81]](#cite_note-81)

Not all hidden camera shows use strictly staged situations. For example, the syndicated program *Cheaters* purports to use hidden cameras to record suspected [cheating](/wiki/Adultery "Adultery") partners, although the authenticity of the show has been questioned, and even refuted by some who have been featured on the series.[[82]](#cite_note-payedtoact-82) Once the evidence has been gathered, the accuser confronts the cheating partner with the assistance of the host. In many special-living documentary programs, hidden cameras are set up all over the residence in order to capture moments missed by the regular camera crew, or intimate bedroom footage.

#### Supernatural and paranormal

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Reality_television&action=edit&section=25 "Edit section: Supernatural and paranormal")]

Further information: [paranormal television](/wiki/Paranormal_television "Paranormal television")

Supernatural and [paranormal](/wiki/Paranormal_TV "Paranormal TV") reality shows such as *[MTV's Fear](/wiki/MTV%27s_Fear "MTV's Fear")*, place participants into frightening situations which ostensibly involve [paranormal](/wiki/Paranormal "Paranormal") phenomena such as [ghosts](/wiki/Ghost "Ghost"), [telekinesis](/wiki/Telekinesis "Telekinesis") or [haunted houses](/wiki/Haunted_house "Haunted house"). In series such as *[Celebrity Paranormal Project](/wiki/Celebrity_Paranormal_Project "Celebrity Paranormal Project")*, the stated aim is investigation, and some series like *[Scariest Places on Earth](/wiki/Scariest_Places_on_Earth "Scariest Places on Earth")* challenge participants to survive the investigation; whereas others such as *[Paranormal State](/wiki/Paranormal_State "Paranormal State")* and *[Ghost Hunters](/wiki/Ghost_Hunters_(TV_series) "Ghost Hunters (TV series)")* use a recurring crew of [paranormal researchers](/wiki/Paranormal_researcher "Paranormal researcher"). In general, the shows follow similar stylized patterns of [night vision](/wiki/Night_vision "Night vision"), surveillance, and hand held camera footage; odd angles; subtitles establishing place and time; desaturated imagery; and non-melodic soundtracks. Noting the trend in reality shows that take the paranormal at face value, *[New York Times](/wiki/The_New_York_Times "The New York Times")* culture editor Mike Hale[[83]](#cite_note-NYTKN-83) characterized [ghost hunting](/wiki/Ghost_hunting "Ghost hunting") shows as "pure theater" and compared the genre to [professional wrestling](/wiki/Professional_wrestling "Professional wrestling") or [softcore pornography](/wiki/Softcore_pornography "Softcore pornography") for its formulaic, teasing approach.[[84]](#cite_note-NYT-84)

### Reality competition or game shows

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Reality_television&action=edit&section=26 "Edit section: Reality competition or game shows")]

Further information: [List of reality television programs](/wiki/List_of_reality_television_programs "List of reality television programs")

|  |  |
| --- | --- |
