Please help [improve this article](/wiki/Special:EditPage/Reality_television "Special:EditPage/Reality television") by [adding citations to reliable sources](/wiki/Help:Referencing_for_beginners "Help:Referencing for beginners") in this section. Unsourced material may be challenged and removed. *(May 2009)* *([Learn how and when to remove this message](/wiki/Help:Maintenance_template_removal "Help:Maintenance template removal"))* |

Another subgenre of reality television is "**reality competition**", "reality [playoffs](/wiki/Playoff "Playoff")", or so-called "reality game shows", which follow the format of non-tournament [elimination](/wiki/Single-elimination_tournament "Single-elimination tournament") contests.[[85]](#cite_note-85) Typically, participants are filmed competing to win a prize, often while living together in a confined environment. In many cases, participants are removed until only one person or team remains, who is then declared the winner. Usually this is done by eliminating participants one at a time (or sometimes two at a time, as an episodic twist due to the number of contestants involved and the length of a given season), through either [disapproval voting](/wiki/Disapproval_voting "Disapproval voting") or by voting for the most popular to win. Voting is done by the viewing audience, the show's own participants, a panel of judges, or some combination of the three.

A well-known example of a reality-competition show is the globally syndicated *Big Brother*, in which cast members live together in the same house, with participants removed at regular intervals by either the viewing audience or, in the American version, by the participants themselves. There remains disagreement over whether talent-search shows such as the *Idol* series, the *Got Talent* series and the *Dancing with the Stars* series are truly reality television or just newer incarnations of shows such as *[Star Search](/wiki/Star_Search "Star Search")*. Although the shows involve a traditional talent search, the shows follow the reality-competition conventions of removing one or more contestants in every episode, allowing the public to vote on who is removed, and interspersing performances with video clips showing the contestants' "back stories", their thoughts about the competition, their rehearsals and unguarded behind-the-scenes moments. Additionally, there is a good deal of unscripted interaction shown between contestants and judges. The American [Primetime Emmy Awards](/wiki/Primetime_Emmy_Awards "Primetime Emmy Awards") have nominated both *American Idol* and *Dancing with the Stars* for the [Outstanding Reality-Competition Program](/wiki/Outstanding_Reality-Competition_Program "Outstanding Reality-Competition Program") Emmy.

Game shows like *Weakest Link*, *Who Wants to Be a Millionaire?*, *[American Gladiators](/wiki/American_Gladiators_(1989_TV_series) "American Gladiators (1989 TV series)")* and *Deal or No Deal*, which were popular in the 2000s, also lie in a gray area: like traditional game shows (e.g., *[The Price Is Right](/wiki/The_Price_Is_Right_(franchise) "The Price Is Right (franchise)")*, *[Jeopardy!](/wiki/Jeopardy! "Jeopardy!")*), the action takes place in an enclosed television studio over a short period of time; however, they have higher production values, more dramatic background music, and higher stakes than traditional shows (done either through putting contestants into physical danger or offering large cash prizes). In addition, there is more interaction between contestants and hosts, and in some cases, they feature reality-style contestant competition or elimination as well. These factors, as well as these shows' rise in global popularity at the same time as the arrival of the reality craze, have led to such shows often being grouped under both the reality television and game show umbrellas.[[86]](#cite_note-86)

There have been various hybrid reality-competition shows, like the worldwide-syndicated *Star Academy*, which combines the *Big Brother* and *Idol* formats, *[The Biggest Loser](/wiki/The_Biggest_Loser "The Biggest Loser")*, which combines competition with the self-improvement format, and *[American Inventor](/wiki/American_Inventor "American Inventor")*, which uses the *Idol* format for products instead of people. Some reality shows that aired mostly during the early 2000s, such as *[Popstars](/wiki/Popstars "Popstars")*, *[Making the Band](/wiki/Making_the_Band "Making the Band")* and *[Project Greenlight](/wiki/Project_Greenlight "Project Greenlight")*, devoted the first part of the season to selecting a winner, and the second part to showing that person or group of people working on a project.

Popular variants of the competition-based format include the following:

#### Dating-based competition

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Reality_television&action=edit&section=27 "Edit section: Dating-based competition")]

Dating-based competition shows follow a contestant choosing one out of a group of suitors. Over the course of either a single episode or an entire season, suitors are eliminated until only the contestant and the final suitor remains. In the early 2000s, this type of reality show dominated the other genres on the major U.S. networks. Examples include *[The Bachelor](/wiki/The_Bachelor_(American_TV_series) "The Bachelor (American TV series)")*, its spin-off *[The Bachelorette](/wiki/The_Bachelorette_(American_TV_series) "The Bachelorette (American TV series)")*, *[Temptation Island](/wiki/Temptation_Island_(TV_series) "Temptation Island (TV series)")*, *[Average Joe](/wiki/Average_Joe_(show) "Average Joe (show)")*, *[Flavor of Love](/wiki/Flavor_of_Love "Flavor of Love")* (a dating show featuring rapper [Flavor Flav](/wiki/Flavor_Flav "Flavor Flav") that led directly and indirectly to over 10 spinoffs), *[The Cougar](/wiki/The_Cougar_(TV_series) "The Cougar (TV series)")* and *[Love in the Wild](/wiki/Love_in_the_Wild "Love in the Wild")*. In *[Married by America](/wiki/Married_by_America "Married by America")*, contestants were chosen by viewer voting. This is one of the older variants of the format; shows such as *[The Dating Game](/wiki/The_Dating_Game "The Dating Game")* that date to the 1960s had similar premises (though each episode was self-contained, and not the serial format of more modern shows). One of the more recent hits was *[Farmer Wants a Wife](/wiki/Farmer_Wants_a_Wife "Farmer Wants a Wife")*.

#### Job search

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Reality_television&action=edit&section=28 "Edit section: Job search")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/6/60/Guy_Fieri_%28cropped%29.jpg)](/wiki/File:Guy_Fieri_(cropped).jpg)

Chef and restaurateur [Guy Fieri](/wiki/Guy_Fieri "Guy Fieri") won the second season of [Food Network](/wiki/Food_Network "Food Network")'s *[Food Network Star](/wiki/Food_Network_Star "Food Network Star")* in 2006, and by 2010 had become "the face of the network".[[87]](#cite_note-87)

In this category, the competition revolves around a skill that contestants were pre-screened for. Competitors perform a variety of tasks based on that skill, are judged, and are then kept or removed by a single expert or a panel of experts. The show is usually presented as a job search of some kind, in which the prize for the winner includes a contract to perform that kind of work and an undisclosed salary, although the award can simply be a sum of money and ancillary prizes, like a cover article in a magazine. The show also features judges who act as counselors, mediators and sometimes mentors to help contestants develop their skills further or perhaps decide their future position in the competition. *Popstars*, which debuted in 1999, may have been the first such show, while the *Idol* series has been the longest-running and, for most of its run, the most popular such franchise. The first job-search show which showed dramatic, unscripted situations may have been *[America's Next Top Model](/wiki/America%27s_Next_Top_Model "America's Next Top Model")*, which premiered in May 2003. Other examples include *[The Apprentice](/wiki/The_Apprentice_(franchise) "The Apprentice (franchise)")* (which judges business skills); *[Hell's Kitchen](/wiki/Hell%27s_Kitchen_(American_TV_series) "Hell's Kitchen (American TV series)")*, *MasterChef* and *[Top Chef](/wiki/Top_Chef "Top Chef")* (for chefs), *[The Great British Bake Off](/wiki/The_Great_British_Bake_Off "The Great British Bake Off")* (for bakers), *[Shear Genius](/wiki/Shear_Genius "Shear Genius")* (for hair styling), *[Project Runway](/wiki/Project_Runway "Project Runway")* (for clothing design), *[Top Design](/wiki/Top_Design "Top Design")* and *[The Great Interior Design Challenge](/wiki/The_Great_Interior_Design_Challenge "The Great Interior Design Challenge")* (for interior design), *[American Dream Builders](/wiki/American_Dream_Builders "American Dream Builders")* (for home builders), *[Stylista](/wiki/Stylista "Stylista")* (for fashion editors), *[Last Comic Standing](/wiki/Last_Comic_Standing "Last Comic Standing")* (for comedians), *[I Know My Kid's a Star](/wiki/I_Know_My_Kid%27s_a_Star "I Know My Kid's a Star")* (for child performers), *[On the Lot](/wiki/On_the_Lot "On the Lot")* (for filmmakers), *[RuPaul's Drag Race](/wiki/RuPaul%27s_Drag_Race "RuPaul's Drag Race")* (for drag queens), *[The Shot](/wiki/The_Shot_(TV_series) "The Shot (TV series)")* (for fashion photographers), *[So You Think You Can Dance](/wiki/So_You_Think_You_Can_Dance "So You Think You Can Dance")* (for dancers), *[MuchMusic VJ Search](/wiki/MuchMusic_VJ_Search "MuchMusic VJ Search")* and *[Food Network Star](/wiki/Food_Network_Star "Food Network Star")* (for television hosts), *[Dream Job](/wiki/Dream_Job "Dream Job")* (for sportscasters), *[American Candidate](/wiki/American_Candidate "American Candidate")* (for aspiring politicians), *[Work of Art](/wiki/Work_of_Art:_The_Next_Great_Artist "Work of Art: The Next Great Artist")* (for artists), *[Face Off](/wiki/Face_Off_(American_TV_series) "Face Off (American TV series)")* (for [prosthetic makeup](/wiki/Prosthetic_makeup "Prosthetic makeup") artists), *[Ink Master](/wiki/Ink_Master "Ink Master")* and *[Best Ink](/wiki/Best_Ink "Best Ink")* (for tattoo artists), *[Platinum Hit](/wiki/Platinum_Hit "Platinum Hit")* (for songwriters), *[Top Shot](/wiki/Top_Shot "Top Shot")* (for marksmen) and *[The Tester](/wiki/The_Tester "The Tester")* (for game testers).

One notable subset, popular from approximately 2005 to 2012, consisted of shows in which the winner gets a specific part in a known film, television show, [musical](/wiki/Musical_theatre "Musical theatre") or performing group. Examples include *[Scream Queens](/wiki/Scream_Queens_(2008_TV_series) "Scream Queens (2008 TV series)")* (where the prize was a role in the *[Saw](/wiki/Saw_(franchise) "Saw (franchise)")* film series), *[The Glee Project](/wiki/The_Glee_Project "The Glee Project")* (for a role on the television show *[Glee](/wiki/Glee_(TV_series) "Glee (TV series)")*) and *[How Do You Solve a Problem like Maria?](/wiki/How_Do_You_Solve_a_Problem_like_Maria%3F "How Do You Solve a Problem like Maria?")* (the lead role in a revival of the musical *[The Sound of Music](/wiki/The_Sound_of_Music "The Sound of Music")*). The most extreme prize for such a show may have been for one of the first such shows, 2005's *[Rock Star: INXS](/wiki/Rock_Star:_INXS "Rock Star: INXS")*, where the winner became the lead singer of the rock band [INXS](/wiki/INXS "INXS"). [J.D. Fortune](/wiki/J.D._Fortune "J.D. Fortune"), who won the show, went on to be INXS's lead singer until 2011.

Some shows use the same format with celebrities: in this case, there is no expectation that the winner will continue this line of work, and prize winnings often go to charity. The most popular such shows have been the *Dancing with the Stars* and *[Dancing on Ice](/wiki/Dancing_on_Ice_around_the_world "Dancing on Ice around the world")* franchises. Other examples of celebrity competition programs include *[Deadline](/wiki/Deadline_(2007_TV_series) "Deadline (2007 TV series)")*, *[Celebracadabra](/wiki/Celebracadabra "Celebracadabra")* and *[Celebrity Apprentice](/wiki/Celebrity_Apprentice "Celebrity Apprentice")*.

#### Different contestants per episode

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Reality_television&action=edit&section=29 "Edit section: Different contestants per episode")]

Some job-related competition shows have a different set of contestants competing on every episode, and thus more closely resemble game shows, although the "confessional" commentary provided by contestants gives them a reality TV aspect. The 1993-1999 Japanese cooking competition *[Iron Chef](/wiki/Iron_Chef "Iron Chef")* could be considered an early example, although it does not include commentary by the participants, only by announcers and judges.

Cooking competition shows with different contestants per episode that are considered reality shows include the *[Chopped](/wiki/Chopped_(TV_series) "Chopped (TV series)")*, *[Come Dine with Me](/wiki/Come_Dine_with_Me "Come Dine with Me")* and *[Nailed It!](/wiki/Nailed_It! "Nailed It!")* franchises, along with *[Cupcake Wars](/wiki/Cupcake_Wars "Cupcake Wars")*, *[Cutthroat Kitchen](/wiki/Cutthroat_Kitchen "Cutthroat Kitchen")*, and *[Guy's Grocery Games](/wiki/Guy%27s_Grocery_Games "Guy's Grocery Games")*. Non-cooking competition shows with a similar format include *[Forged in Fire](/wiki/Forged_in_Fire "Forged in Fire")* (whose producer cited [Food Network](/wiki/Food_Network "Food Network")'s competition programs as a direct inspiration),[[88]](#cite_note-4esquire-88) and *[The Butcher](/wiki/The_Butcher_(TV_series) "The Butcher (TV series)")*.

#### Immunity

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Reality_television&action=edit&section=30 "Edit section: Immunity")]

One concept pioneered by, and unique to, reality competition shows is the idea of immunity, in which a contestant can win the right to be exempt the next time contestants are eliminated from the show. Possibly the first instance of immunity in reality TV was on *[Survivor](/wiki/Survivor_(TV_series) "Survivor (TV series)")*, which premiered in 1997 in Sweden as *[Expedition Robinson](/wiki/Expedition_Robinson "Expedition Robinson")*, before gaining international prominence after the American edition (titled *[Survivor](/wiki/Survivor_(American_TV_series) "Survivor (American TV series)")*) premiered in 2000. On that show, there are complex rules around immunity: a player can achieve it by winning challenges (either as a team in the tribal phase or individually in the merged phase), or, in more recent seasons, through finding [a hidden totem](/wiki/Survivor_(TV_series)#Hidden_immunity_idols "Survivor (TV series)"). They can also pass on their immunity to someone else and in the latter case, they can keep their immunity secret from other players.[[89]](#cite_note-89)

On most shows, immunity is quite a bit simpler: it is usually achieved by winning a task, often a relatively minor task during the first half of the episode; the announcement of immunity is made publicly and immunity is usually non-transferable. At some point in the season, immunity ceases to be available, and all contestants are susceptible to elimination. Competition shows that have featured immunity include the *Apprentice*, *Big Brother*, *Biggest Loser*, *Top Model*, *Project Runway*, *Lego Masters*, and *Top Chef* franchises. Immunity may come with additional power as well, such as in the American version of *Big Brother* where the winning contestant usually has influence over deciding who faces an elimination vote later in the week. In one *Apprentice* episode, a participant chose to waive his earned immunity and was immediately "fired" for giving up this "powerful asset".[[90]](#cite_note-90)

### Sports

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Reality_television&action=edit&section=31 "Edit section: Sports")]

[Sport](/wiki/Sport "Sport")-related reality shows can fall within the aforementioned sub-genres, either using it as the basis of competition, or by following sport as a profession:

* Competition-based programs, featuring groups of athletes competing against each other in challenges and events within a specific sport, such as [athletics](/wiki/Sport_of_athletics "Sport of athletics") (*[Ninja Warrior](/wiki/Ninja_Warrior_(franchise) "Ninja Warrior (franchise)")*, *[Exatlon](/wiki/Exatlon "Exatlon")*), [golf](/wiki/Golf "Golf") (*[The Big Break](/wiki/The_Big_Break "The Big Break")*), [auto racing](/wiki/Auto_racing "Auto racing") (*[Crash Course](/wiki/Crash_Course_(game_show) "Crash Course (game show)")*, *[Hyperdrive](/wiki/Hyperdrive_(American_TV_series) "Hyperdrive (American TV series)")*, *[Pinks](/wiki/Pinks_(TV_series) "Pinks (TV series)")*), and [combat sports](/wiki/Combat_sports "Combat sports") (*[The Contender](/wiki/The_Contender_(TV_series) "The Contender (TV series)")*, *[The Ultimate Fighter](/wiki/The_Ultimate_Fighter "The Ultimate Fighter")*) for example. In the case of combat sports examples, the [UFC](/wiki/UFC "UFC")-produced [mixed martial arts](/wiki/Mixed_martial_arts "Mixed martial arts") competition series *The Ultimate Fighter*, and [WWE](/wiki/WWE "WWE")'s [professional wrestling](/wiki/Professional_wrestling "Professional wrestling") talent searches *[Tough Enough](/wiki/WWE_Tough_Enough "WWE Tough Enough")*, *[Diva Search](/wiki/WWE_Diva_Search "WWE Diva Search")*, *[NXT](/wiki/WWE_NXT_seasons_1%E2%80%935 "WWE NXT seasons 1–5")* (before it was reformatted as a traditional wrestling show with developmental talent) and *[LFG](/wiki/WWE_LFG "WWE LFG")*, a contract with the respective organization was the grand prize.
  + Some series may follow non-sportspeople (usually celebrities, or in some cases athletes known for their participation in a different sport) training and participating in a sporting event, such as *[The Games](/wiki/The_Games_(British_TV_series) "The Games (British TV series)")*, Irish series *[Celebrity Bainisteoir](/wiki/Celebrity_Bainisteoir "Celebrity Bainisteoir")* (where celebrities are tasked to become the [managers](/wiki/Manager_(Gaelic_games) "Manager (Gaelic games)") of mid-level [Gaelic football](/wiki/Gaelic_football "Gaelic football") teams), and *Dancing on Ice* and *[Battle of the Blades](/wiki/Battle_of_the_Blades "Battle of the Blades")* ([figure skating](/wiki/Figure_skating "Figure skating") competitions with similarities to *Dancing with the Stars*).
* Documentary-style series following specific competitions, teams, or athletes, such as *[Knight School](/wiki/Knight_School_(American_TV_series) "Knight School (American TV series)")* ([Texas Tech Raiders men's basketball](/wiki/Texas_Tech_Red_Raiders_basketball "Texas Tech Red Raiders basketball")) *[Hard Knocks](/wiki/Hard_Knocks_(documentary_series) "Hard Knocks (documentary series)")* ([NFL](/wiki/National_Football_League "National Football League")), *[All or Nothing](/wiki/All_or_Nothing_(franchise) "All or Nothing (franchise)")*, and *[Drive to Survive](/wiki/Formula_1:_Drive_to_Survive "Formula 1: Drive to Survive")* ([Formula One](/wiki/Formula_One "Formula One")).
* Docusoaps following the lives of athletes and/or their families, such as *[Total Divas](/wiki/Total_Divas "Total Divas")* ([women in WWE](/wiki/Women_in_WWE "Women in WWE")) and *[WAGS](/wiki/WAGS_(TV_series) "WAGS (TV series)")* (wives and girlfriends of sportspeople).

### Parodies and hoaxes

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Reality_television&action=edit&section=32 "Edit section: Parodies and hoaxes")]

Some reality shows aim to [satirize](/wiki/Satire "Satire") and deconstruct the conventions and cliches of the genre for comedic effect; in such cases, a fictitious premise is usually presented to one or more of the participants, with the rest of the cast consisting of actors and other figures that are in on the joke.

* *[The Joe Schmo Show](/wiki/The_Joe_Schmo_Show "The Joe Schmo Show")*, a series in which one or more civilians are set up as contestants on a [fictitious reality competition](/wiki/Story_within_a_story "Story within a story"), with the remaining "contestants" being actors playing characters representing stereotypical [archetypes](/wiki/Archetype "Archetype") of reality television contestants.[[91]](#cite_note-post-gazette.com-91) The first season portrayed a *Big Brother*-like show entitled *Lap of Luxury*, with subsequent seasons featuring the dating show *Last Chance for Love* (which featured both a man and woman as the targets) and a job hunt competition in the field of [bounty hunting](/wiki/Bounty_hunter "Bounty hunter") (*Full Bounty*; its broadcaster [Spike](/wiki/Paramount_Network "Paramount Network") concealed the third season by announcing *Full Bounty* within a slate of new reality series in production for the channel, without immediately revealing it was actually a *Joe Schmo Show* revival).[[91]](#cite_note-post-gazette.com-91)
* *[My Big Fat Obnoxious Boss](/wiki/My_Big_Fat_Obnoxious_Boss "My Big Fat Obnoxious Boss")*, a parody of *The Apprentice* in which the contestants were given challenges with inane objectives by businessman Mr. N. Paul Todd (an [anagram](/wiki/Anagram "Anagram") of *Apprentice* host [Donald Trump](/wiki/Donald_Trump "Donald Trump")). The final decision on eliminations in each episode was always given to Todd's "real boss"—revealed in the series finale to have been a [chimpanzee](/wiki/Chimpanzee "Chimpanzee") spinning a wheel with the contestants' names on it.[[92]](#cite_note-92)[[93]](#cite_note-93)[[94]](#cite_note-94)
* *[Superstar USA](/wiki/Superstar_USA "Superstar USA")*, a parody of *American Idol* attempting to find the worst singer. The judges criticized good singers and eliminated them, but bad singers were praised and allowed to progress further through the competition.[[95]](#cite_note-95)
* *[Space Cadets](/wiki/Space_Cadets_(TV_series) "Space Cadets (TV series)")*, a series in which a group of contestants were set up on the purported reality competition series *Thrill Seekers*, where they would allegedly receive [astronaut training](/wiki/Astronaut_training "Astronaut training") in Russia and compete to become Britain's first [space tourists](/wiki/Space_tourists "Space tourists").[[96]](#cite_note-guardian-96)
* *[I Wanna Marry "Harry"](/wiki/I_Wanna_Marry_%22Harry%22 "I Wanna Marry \"Harry\"")*, a hoax dating competition where single women were manipulated into believing they were competing for the affection of [Prince Harry](/wiki/Prince_Harry "Prince Harry"), but in reality "Harry" was actually a lookalike.[[97]](#cite_note-97)
* *[Nathan for You](/wiki/Nathan_for_You "Nathan for You")*, a reality [mockumentary](/wiki/Mockumentary "Mockumentary") in which [Nathan Fielder](/wiki/Nathan_Fielder "Nathan Fielder") (as an exaggerated and socially awkward version of himself) attempts to help struggling businesses, but employs unusual and outlandish strategies to do so—such as rebranding a coffee shop as a [parody](/wiki/Parody "Parody") of the [Starbucks](/wiki/Starbucks "Starbucks") chain in which [everything is prepended with the word "dumb"](/wiki/Dumb_Starbucks "Dumb Starbucks"), and trying to make the lifting of boxes into an exercise [fad](/wiki/Fad "Fad") so the owner of a [moving company](/wiki/Moving_company "Moving company") could receive free labor under the guise of marketing himself as a [personal trainer](/wiki/Personal_trainer "Personal trainer").[[98]](#cite_note-lat-dumbstarbucks-98)[[99]](#cite_note-Alston-99) Although aware they were on a reality program, the employees of the businesses featured were unaware of the show's comedic nature, and reacted genuinely to Fielder's antics.[[99]](#cite_note-Alston-99)[[100]](#cite_note-gq15-100) On multiple occasions, the show received media attention related to its stunts prior to broadcast.[[99]](#cite_note-Alston-99)[[98]](#cite_note-lat-dumbstarbucks-98)
  + In 2022, Fielder premiered a spiritual successor to the series for [HBO](/wiki/HBO "HBO"), *[The Rehearsal](/wiki/The_Rehearsal_(TV_series) "The Rehearsal (TV series)")*, which follows him helping individuals "rehearse" for difficult social interactions and life events, and manipulating subjects into increasingly outlandish scenarios as an exaggerated version of himself.[[101]](#cite_note-Vulture-101)[[102]](#cite_note-Vulture2-102)[[103]](#cite_note-103)[[104]](#cite_note-vulture-profile-104)
* The Dutch reality show *[De Grote Donorshow](/wiki/De_Grote_Donorshow "De Grote Donorshow")*—where a group of patients competed to receive a [kidney donation](/wiki/Kidney_transplantation "Kidney transplantation") from a terminally-ill woman—was, by contrast, not intended for comedic effect, and was a hoax directed at viewers to help raise awareness for kidney donation.[[105]](#cite_note-105)
* *[Jury Duty](/wiki/Jury_Duty_(2023_TV_series) "Jury Duty (2023 TV series)")*, a mockumentary series portraying a fictional [jury trial](/wiki/Juries_in_the_United_States "Juries in the United States") in which one member of the jury is not aware that the entire trial and its events are planned and acted out.[[106]](#cite_note-106)
* *The Underdog: Josh Must Win*, a 2024 British series in which a cast of contestants are set up on a *Big Brother*-like show entitled *The Favourite*. However, a group of celebrities (themselves alumni of other reality shows) are tasked with manipulating the show from behind the scenes to ensure that Josh—a contestant who is portrayed as a contrasting underdog to the rest of the cast—wins the competition. If successful, all ten contestants would get to split the final prize money.[[107]](#cite_note-107)

## Criticism and analysis

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Reality_television&action=edit&section=33 "Edit section: Criticism and analysis")]

Further information: [Criticism of reality television](/wiki/Criticism_of_reality_television "Criticism of reality television")

### "Reality" as misnomer

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Reality_television&action=edit&section=34 "Edit section: \"Reality\" as misnomer")]

The authenticity of reality television is often called into question by its detractors. The genre's title of "reality" is often criticized as being inaccurate because of claims that the genre frequently includes elements such as premeditated scripting (including a practice called "[soft-scripting](/wiki/Soft-scripting "Soft-scripting")"), acting, urgings from behind-the-scenes crew to create specified situations of adversity and drama, and misleading editing. It has often been described as "scripting without paper".

In many cases, the entire premise of the show is contrived, based around a competition or another unusual situation. Some shows have been accused of using fakery in order to create more compelling television, such as having premeditated storylines and in some cases feeding participants lines of dialogue, focusing only on participants' most outlandish behavior, and altering events through editing and re-shoots.[[108]](#cite_note-washingtonpost-108)[[109]](#cite_note-MSNBC-109)

Shows such as *Survivor* and *Amazing Race* that offer a monetary prize are regulated in the United States by federal "game show" law, [47 U.S.C.](/wiki/Title_47_of_the_United_States_Code "Title 47 of the United States Code") [§ 509](https://www.law.cornell.edu/uscode/text/47/509), and are monitored during the filming by the legal staff and standards and practice staff of the parent network. These shows cannot be manipulated in any way that affects the outcome of the game. However, misleading editing does not fall into altering the fairness of the competition.

Beyond concerns about authenticity, media critics have argued that reality television may have broader societal consequences. In 2022, Time magazine TV critic Judy Berman wrote that "to the extent that the U.S. has become a harsher, shallower, angrier, more divided place in the 21st century, reality TV, which has helped normalize cruelty, belligerence, superficiality, and disloyalty, and rewarded people who weaponize those traits, bears a share of the blame."[[110]](#cite_note-110)

Reality television shows that have been accused of, or admitted to, deception include *[The Real World](/wiki/The_Real_World "The Real World")*,[[111]](#cite_note-EW-111)[[112]](#cite_note-112)[[113]](#cite_note-Westword-113) the American version of *[Survivor](/wiki/Survivor_(American_TV_series) "Survivor (American TV series)")*,[[114]](#cite_note-TodayMSNBC-114) *[Joe Millionaire](/wiki/Joe_Millionaire "Joe Millionaire")*,[[115]](#cite_note-115) *[The Hills](/wiki/The_Hills_(TV_series) "The Hills (TV series)")*, *[A Shot at Love with Tila Tequila](/wiki/A_Shot_at_Love_with_Tila_Tequila "A Shot at Love with Tila Tequila")*,[[116]](#cite_note-116) *[Hogan Knows Best](/wiki/Hogan_Knows_Best "Hogan Knows Best")*,[[117]](#cite_note-117) *[Extreme Makeover: Home Edition](/wiki/Extreme_Makeover:_Home_Edition "Extreme Makeover: Home Edition")*,[[118]](#cite_note-118) *[The Bachelor](/wiki/The_Bachelor_(American_TV_series) "The Bachelor (American TV series)")* and *[The Bachelorette](/wiki/The_Bachelorette_(American_TV_series) "The Bachelorette (American TV series)")*,[[119]](#cite_note-119)[[120]](#cite_note-120) *[Pawn Stars](/wiki/Pawn_Stars "Pawn Stars")*,[[121]](#cite_note-AutoBio-121) *[Storage Wars](/wiki/Storage_Wars "Storage Wars")*,[[122]](#cite_note-122) *[Squid Game: The Challenge](/wiki/Squid_Game:_The_Challenge "Squid Game: The Challenge")* and *[Keeping Up with the Kardashians](/wiki/Keeping_Up_with_the_Kardashians "Keeping Up with the Kardashians")*.[[123]](#cite_note-123)[[124]](#cite_note-124)

In contrast, critic [Matt Zoller Seitz](/wiki/Matt_Zoller_Seitz "Matt Zoller Seitz") praised *[Deadliest Catch](/wiki/Deadliest_Catch "Deadliest Catch")* as having "brought old-school documentary sobriety to a genre more often known for shamelessness."[[125]](#cite_note-125)

### Political and cultural impact

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Reality_television&action=edit&section=35 "Edit section: Political and cultural impact")]

Reality television's global success has become, in the view of some analysts, an important political phenomenon. In some[*[quantify](/wiki/Wikipedia:Manual_of_Style/Dates_and_numbers "Wikipedia:Manual of Style/Dates and numbers")*] [authoritarian](/wiki/Authoritarian "Authoritarian") countries, reality-television voting has provided the first opportunity for many citizens to vote in any free and fair wide-scale "elections". In addition, the frankness of the settings on some reality shows presents situations that are often taboo in certain conservative cultures, like *[Star Academy Arab World](/wiki/Star_Academy_Arab_World "Star Academy Arab World")*, which began airing in 2003, and which shows male and female contestants living together.[[126]](#cite_note-126) A Pan-Arab version of *Big Brother* was cancelled in 2004 after less than two weeks on the air after a public outcry and street protests.[[127]](#cite_note-127) In 2004 journalist [Matt Labash](/wiki/Matt_Labash "Matt Labash"), noting both of these issues, wrote that "the best hope of little Americas developing in the Middle East could be Arab-produced reality TV".[[128]](#cite_note-128)

In 2007, [Abu Dhabi TV](/wiki/Abu_Dhabi_TV "Abu Dhabi TV") began airing *[Million's Poet](/wiki/Million%27s_Poet "Million's Poet")*, a show featuring *[Pop Idol](/wiki/Pop_Idol "Pop Idol")*-style voting and elimination, but for the writing and oration of [Arabic poetry](/wiki/Arabic_poetry "Arabic poetry"). The show became popular in Arab countries, with around 18 million viewers,[[129]](#cite_note-Winner's_tribe-129) partly because it was able to combine the excitement of reality television with a traditional, culturally relevant topic.[[130]](#cite_note-130) In April 2010, however, the show also became a subject of political controversy, when [Hissa Hilal](/wiki/Hissa_Hilal "Hissa Hilal"), a 43-year-old female [Saudi](/wiki/Saudi_people "Saudi people") competitor, read out a poem criticizing her country's Muslim clerics.[[131]](#cite_note-131) Both critics and the public reacted favorably to Hilal's poetry; she received the highest scores from the judges throughout the competition and came in third place overall.[[129]](#cite_note-Winner's_tribe-129)

In [India](/wiki/India "India"), in the summer of 2007, coverage of the third season of *[Indian Idol](/wiki/Indian_Idol "Indian Idol")* focused on the breaking down of cultural and socioeconomic barriers as the public rallied around the show's top two contestants.[[31]](#cite_note-Reality_TV_and_Participatory_Culture_in_India-31)

The [Chinese](/wiki/China "China") singing competition *[Super Girl](/wiki/Super_Girl_(TV_series) "Super Girl (TV series)")* (a local imitation of *Pop Idol*) has similarly been cited[*[by whom?](/wiki/Wikipedia:Manual_of_Style/Words_to_watch#Unsupported_attributions "Wikipedia:Manual of Style/Words to watch")*] for its political and cultural impact.[[132]](#cite_note-Becoming_Extra-Ordinary-132) After the finale of the show's 2005 season drew an audience of around 400 million people, and eight million [text-message](/wiki/Text-message "Text-message") votes, the state-run English-language newspaper *[Beijing Today](/wiki/Beijing_Today "Beijing Today")* ran the front-page headline: "Is Super Girl a Force for Democracy?"[[133]](#cite_note-133) The Chinese government criticized the show, citing both its democratic nature and its excessive vulgarity, or "worldliness",[[134]](#cite_note-134) and in 2006 banned it outright.[[135]](#cite_note-135) It was later reintroduced[*[by whom?](/wiki/Wikipedia:Manual_of_Style/Words_to_watch#Unsupported_attributions "Wikipedia:Manual of Style/Words to watch")*] in 2009, before being banned again in 2011. *Super Girl* has also been criticized by non-government commentators for creating seemingly impossible ideals that may be harmful to Chinese youth.[[132]](#cite_note-Becoming_Extra-Ordinary-132)

In [Indonesia](/wiki/Indonesia "Indonesia"), reality television shows have surpassed soap operas as the most-watched broadcast programs.[[136]](#cite_note-Indonesia-136) One popular program, *Jika Aku Menjadi* ("If I Were"), follows young, middle-class people as they are temporarily placed into lower-class life, where they learn to appreciate their circumstances back home by experiencing daily life for the less fortunate.[[136]](#cite_note-Indonesia-136) Critics have claimed that this and similar programs in Indonesia reinforce traditionally Western ideals of [materialism](/wiki/Materialism "Materialism") and [consumerism](/wiki/Consumerism "Consumerism").[[136]](#cite_note-Indonesia-136) However, Eko Nugroho, reality-show producer and president of Dreamlight World Media, insists that these reality shows are not promoting American lifestyles but rather reaching people through their universal desires.[[136]](#cite_note-Indonesia-136)

Reality television has also received criticism in Britain and the United States for its [ideological](/wiki/Ideological "Ideological") relationship with [surveillance societies](/wiki/Surveillance_societies "Surveillance societies") and consumerism. Writing in *The New York Times* in 2012, author Mark Andrejevic characterised the role of reality television in a post-9/11 society as the normalisation of surveillance in participatory monitoring, the "logic of the emerging surveillance economy", and in the promise of a societal self-image that is contrived.[[137]](#cite_note-andrejevic1-137) An [LSE](/wiki/London_School_of_Economics "London School of Economics") paper by Nick Couldry associates reality television with [neoliberalism](/wiki/Neoliberalism "Neoliberalism"), condemning the ritualised enactment and consumption of what must be legitimised for the society it serves.[[138]](#cite_note-couldry1-138)

### As a substitute for scripted drama

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Reality_television&action=edit&section=36 "Edit section: As a substitute for scripted drama")]

Reality television generally costs less to produce than scripted series.[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*]

VH1 executive vice president Michael Hirschorn wrote in 2007 that the plots and subject matters on reality television are more authentic and more engaging than in scripted dramas, writing that scripted network television "remains dominated by variants on the [police procedural](/wiki/Police_procedural "Police procedural")... in which a stock group of characters (ethnically, sexually, and generationally diverse) grapples with endless versions of the same dilemma. The episodes have all the ritual predictability of Japanese [Noh](/wiki/Noh "Noh") theater," while reality television is "the liveliest genre on the set right now. It has engaged hot-button cultural issues – class, sex, race – that respectable television... rarely touches."[[139]](#cite_note-139)

[Television critic](/wiki/Television_critic "Television critic") [James Poniewozik](/wiki/James_Poniewozik "James Poniewozik") wrote in 2008 that reality shows like *Deadliest Catch* and *[Ice Road Truckers](/wiki/Ice_Road_Truckers "Ice Road Truckers")* showcase working-class people of the kind that "used to be routine" on scripted network television, but that became a rarity in the 2000s: "The better to woo upscale viewers, TV has evicted its mechanics and dockworkers to collect higher rents from yuppies in coffeehouses."[[140]](#cite_note-140)

In a 2021 interview, filmmaker [Mike White](/wiki/Mike_White_(filmmaker) "Mike White (filmmaker)") (who had previously competed on *The Amazing Race* and *Survivor*) said that reality competition shows like *Survivor* accurately conveyed how, in real life, "so much of self is situational", so that, as circumstances change, "the oppressed becomes the oppressor, the bully becomes the bullied." In contrast, he felt that in scripted drama "there's a lot of religiosity around humanity."[[141]](#cite_note-141)

### Instant celebrity

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Reality_television&action=edit&section=37 "Edit section: Instant celebrity")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/c/c6/One_Direction_at_the_Logies_Awards_2012.jpg/250px-One_Direction_at_the_Logies_Awards_2012.jpg)](/wiki/File:One_Direction_at_the_Logies_Awards_2012.jpg)

The English-Irish [boy band](/wiki/Boy_band "Boy band") [One Direction](/wiki/One_Direction "One Direction") formed during the [seventh series](/wiki/The_X_Factor_(British_series_7) "The X Factor (British series 7)") of the British singing competition *[The X Factor](/wiki/The_X_Factor_(British_TV_series) "The X Factor (British TV series)")* in 2010, and later became one of the [best-selling boy bands](/wiki/Boy_band#Best-selling_boy_bands "Boy band") of all time.

Reality television has the potential to turn its participants into national [celebrities](/wiki/Celebrity "Celebrity"), at least for a short period. This is most notable in talent-search programs such as *Idol* and *The X Factor*, which have spawned music stars in many of the countries in which they have aired. Many other shows, however, have made mostly temporary celebrities out of their participants; some participants have then been able to parlay this fame into media and merchandising careers. Participants of non-talent-search programs who have had subsequent acting careers include [Lilian Afegbai](/wiki/Lilian_Afegbai "Lilian Afegbai"), [Jacinda Barrett](/wiki/Jacinda_Barrett "Jacinda Barrett"), [Jamie Chung](/wiki/Jamie_Chung "Jamie Chung"), [Stephen Colletti](/wiki/Stephen_Colletti "Stephen Colletti"), [David Giuntoli](/wiki/David_Giuntoli "David Giuntoli"), [Vishal Karwal](/wiki/Vishal_Karwal "Vishal Karwal"), [NeNe Leakes](/wiki/NeNe_Leakes "NeNe Leakes") and [Angela Trimbur](/wiki/Angela_Trimbur "Angela Trimbur"); though Barrett and Trimbur were already aspiring actresses when they appeared on reality television. Reality TV participants who have become television hosts and personalities include [Nabilla Benattia](/wiki/Nabilla_Benattia "Nabilla Benattia"), [Rachel Campos-Duffy](/wiki/Rachel_Campos-Duffy "Rachel Campos-Duffy"), [Kristin Cavallari](/wiki/Kristin_Cavallari "Kristin Cavallari"), [Colby Donaldson](/wiki/Colby_Donaldson "Colby Donaldson"), [Raffaella Fico](/wiki/Raffaella_Fico "Raffaella Fico"), [Elisabeth Hasselbeck](/wiki/Elisabeth_Hasselbeck "Elisabeth Hasselbeck"), [Katie Hopkins](/wiki/Katie_Hopkins "Katie Hopkins"), [Rebecca Jarvis](/wiki/Rebecca_Jarvis "Rebecca Jarvis"), [Jodie Marsh](/wiki/Jodie_Marsh "Jodie Marsh"), [Heidi Montag](/wiki/Heidi_Montag "Heidi Montag"), [Tiffany Pollard](/wiki/Tiffany_Pollard "Tiffany Pollard") and [Whitney Port](/wiki/Whitney_Port "Whitney Port"); some of them have had acting careers as well. Reality TV participants who have become television personalities as well as successful entrepreneurs include [Gemma Collins](/wiki/Gemma_Collins "Gemma Collins"), [Lauren Conrad](/wiki/Lauren_Conrad "Lauren Conrad"), [Jade Goody](/wiki/Jade_Goody "Jade Goody"), [Bethenny Frankel](/wiki/Bethenny_Frankel "Bethenny Frankel") and [Spencer Matthews](/wiki/Spencer_Matthews "Spencer Matthews"). Several cast members of MTV's *[Jersey Shore](/wiki/Jersey_Shore_(TV_series) "Jersey Shore (TV series)")* have had lucrative endorsement deals, and in some cases their own product lines. Wrestlers [Mike "The Miz" Mizanin](/wiki/The_Miz "The Miz") and [David Otunga](/wiki/David_Otunga "David Otunga") got their start on non-athletic reality shows.

In [Australia](/wiki/Australia "Australia"), various reality TV personalities have later served as radio hosts, including Fitzy and Rachel Corbett from *[Big Brother](/wiki/Big_Brother_(Australian_TV_series) "Big Brother (Australian TV series)")*,[[142]](#cite_note-142) Mick Newell from *[My Kitchen Rules](/wiki/My_Kitchen_Rules_(series_4) "My Kitchen Rules (series 4)")*,[[143]](#cite_note-143) Heather Maltman from *[The Bachelor](/wiki/The_Bachelor_(Australian_TV_series) "The Bachelor (Australian TV series)")*,[[144]](#cite_note-144) and Sam Frost from *[The Bachelorette](/wiki/The_Bachelorette_(Australian_TV_series) "The Bachelorette (Australian TV series)")*.[[145]](#cite_note-145)[[146]](#cite_note-146)

Some reality-television alumni have parlayed their fame into paid public appearances.[[147]](#cite_note-Aurthur-147)[[148]](#cite_note-CNNMoney-148)

Several [socialites](/wiki/Socialite "Socialite"), or children of famous parents, who were somewhat well known before they appeared on reality television shows have become much more famous as a result, including [Paris Hilton](/wiki/Paris_Hilton "Paris Hilton"), [Nicole Richie](/wiki/Nicole_Richie "Nicole Richie"), [Kelly Osbourne](/wiki/Kelly_Osbourne "Kelly Osbourne"), [Kim Kardashian](/wiki/Kim_Kardashian "Kim Kardashian"), and many of the rest of the [Kardashian](/wiki/Kardashian "Kardashian") family.

Reality television personalities often get derided as "[Z-list](/wiki/A-list "A-list") celebrities". Some have been lampooned for exploiting an undeserved "[15 minutes of fame](/wiki/15_minutes_of_fame "15 minutes of fame")".[[149]](#cite_note-InsideEdition-149) The [Kardashian family](/wiki/Kardashian_family "Kardashian family") is one such group of reality television personalities who were subject to this criticism in the 2010s,[[149]](#cite_note-InsideEdition-149)[[150]](#cite_note-150) Kim Kardashian in particular.[[151]](#cite_note-151)

### Springboard for political success

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Reality_television&action=edit&section=38 "Edit section: Springboard for political success")]

Two international franchises, *[The Apprentice](/wiki/The_Apprentice_(franchise) "The Apprentice (franchise)")* and *[Dragons' Den](/wiki/Dragons%27_Den "Dragons' Den")*, are notable for having some of the business people who appeared there as judges and investors go on to win political office. The prime example is [President of the United States](/wiki/President_of_the_United_States "President of the United States") [Donald Trump](/wiki/Donald_Trump "Donald Trump"): his stint as host of the original *[The Apprentice](/wiki/The_Apprentice_(American_TV_series) "The Apprentice (American TV series)")* from 2004 to 2015 has been credited by some commentators as a factor in his political success, since it greatly increased his fame, and showcased him as a tough and experienced authority figure.[[152]](#cite_note-152) [Lado Gurgenidze](/wiki/Lado_Gurgenidze "Lado Gurgenidze"), who hosted the Georgian version of *The Apprentice* in 2005, was appointed [Prime Minister of Georgia](/wiki/Prime_Minister_of_Georgia "Prime Minister of Georgia") from 2007, and served until 2008. [Harry Harkimo](/wiki/Harry_Harkimo "Harry Harkimo"), who hosted the Finnish version of *The Apprentice* from 2009 to 2013, has been a member of the [Parliament of Finland](/wiki/Parliament_of_Finland "Parliament of Finland") since 2015. [João Doria](/wiki/Jo%C3%A3o_Doria "João Doria"), who hosted seasons 7-8 of the Brazilian version of *The Apprentice*, *O Aprendiz*, from 2010 to 2011, served as [Mayor of São Paulo](/wiki/Mayor_of_S%C3%A3o_Paulo "Mayor of São Paulo") from 2017 to 2018,[[153]](#cite_note-153) and as [Governor of São Paulo](/wiki/Governor_of_S%C3%A3o_Paulo "Governor of São Paulo") from 2018 to 2022. [Bruno Bonnell](/wiki/Bruno_Bonnell "Bruno Bonnell"), who hosted the short-lived French version of *The Apprentice* in 2015, was a member of France's [National Assembly](/wiki/National_Assembly_(France) "National Assembly (France)") from 2017 to 2022.

*Dragons' Den* investors who have gone on to hold political office after appearing on their country's version of the program include [Tommy Ahlers](/wiki/Tommy_Ahlers "Tommy Ahlers") of Denmark, [Nir Barkat](/wiki/Nir_Barkat "Nir Barkat") of Israel, [Anne Berner](/wiki/Anne_Berner "Anne Berner") of Finland, [Tomio Okamura](/wiki/Tomio_Okamura "Tomio Okamura") of the Czech Republic, and [Lencke Wischhusen](/wiki/Lencke_Wischhusen "Lencke Wischhusen") of Germany.

In a rare case of a previously unknown reality television alumnus succeeding in the political arena, *[The Real World: Boston](/wiki/The_Real_World:_Boston "The Real World: Boston")* cast member [Sean Duffy](/wiki/Sean_Duffy "Sean Duffy") was a [U.S. Representative](/wiki/U.S._Representative "U.S. Representative") from [Wisconsin](/wiki/Wisconsin "Wisconsin") from 2010 to 2019.[[154]](#cite_note-154)

### Youth audience

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Reality_television&action=edit&section=39 "Edit section: Youth audience")]

In 2006, four of the ten most popular programs among viewers under 17 were reality shows.[[155]](#cite_note-Appeal_of_Reality_Television-155) Studies have shown that young people emulate the behavior displayed on these programs, gathering much of their knowledge of the social world, particularly about consumer practices, from television.[[132]](#cite_note-Becoming_Extra-Ordinary-132)[[156]](#cite_note-New_Media_Practices_in_China-156)[[157]](#cite_note-Media_Consumption_and_Global_Visions-157)[[158]](#cite_note-It's_About_Audience-158) Some critics have decried the positive representation of sexually objectified women in shows like *[The Girls Next Door](/wiki/The_Girls_Next_Door "The Girls Next Door")*.[[159]](#cite_note-159)[[160]](#cite_note-160)

In 2007, according to the [Learning and Skills Council](/wiki/Learning_and_Skills_Council "Learning and Skills Council"), one in seven UK teenagers hoped to gain fame by appearing on reality television.[[161]](#cite_note-161)

### Appeal

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Reality_television&action=edit&section=40 "Edit section: Appeal")]

A number of studies have tried to pinpoint the appeal of reality television.[[162]](#cite_note-162) Factors that have been cited in its appeal include personal identification with the onscreen participants; pure entertainment; diversion from scripted TV; vicarious participation;[[163]](#cite_note-163) a feeling of self-importance compared to onscreen participants;[[164]](#cite_note-Reiss,_S._2004-164) enjoyment of competition;[[164]](#cite_note-Reiss,_S._2004-164) and an appeal to [voyeurism](/wiki/Voyeurism "Voyeurism"), especially given "scenes which take place in private settings, contain [nudity](/wiki/Nudity "Nudity"), or include gossip".[[165]](#cite_note-165)[[166]](#cite_note-166)[[167]](#cite_note-167)

A 2012 survey by [Today.com](/wiki/Today_(American_TV_program) "Today (American TV program)") found that Americans who watch reality television regularly are more extroverted, more neurotic, and have lower self-esteem than those who do not.[[168]](#cite_note-168)

## Similar works in popular culture

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Reality_television&action=edit&section=41 "Edit section: Similar works in popular culture")]

A number of fictional works since the 1940s have contained elements similar to elements of reality television. They tended to be set in a [dystopian](/wiki/Dystopia "Dystopia") future, with subjects being recorded against their will and often involved violence.

* "The Seventh Victim" (1953) is a short story by science fiction author [Robert Sheckley](/wiki/Robert_Sheckley "Robert Sheckley") that depicted a futuristic game in which one player gets to hunt down another player and kill him. The first player who can score ten kills wins the grand prize. This story was the basis for the Italian film *[The 10th Victim](/wiki/The_10th_Victim "The 10th Victim")* (1965).
* *[You're Another](/wiki/You%27re_Another "You're Another")*, a 1955 short story by [Damon Knight](/wiki/Damon_Knight "Damon Knight"), is about a man who discovers that he is an actor in a "livie", a live-action show that is viewed by billions of people in the future.
* *[A King in New York](/wiki/A_King_in_New_York "A King in New York")*, a 1957 film written and directed by [Charlie Chaplin](/wiki/Charlie_Chaplin "Charlie Chaplin") has the main character, a fictional European monarch portrayed by Chaplin, secretly filmed while talking to people at a New York cocktail party. The footage is later turned into a television show within the film.
* "The Prize of Peril"[[169]](#cite_note-169) (1958), another Robert Sheckley story, is about a television show in which a contestant volunteers to be hunted for a week by trained killers, with a large cash prize if he survives. It was adapted in 1970 as the TV movie *Das Millionenspiel*, and again in 1983 as the movie *[Le Prix du Danger](/wiki/Le_Prix_du_Danger "Le Prix du Danger")*.
* [Richard G. Stern](/wiki/Richard_G._Stern "Richard G. Stern")'s novel *Golk* (1960) is about a hidden-camera show similar to *Candid Camera*.
* "It Could Be You" (1964), a short story by Australian Frank Roberts, features a day-in-day-out televised blood sport.
* "Survivor" (1965), a science fiction novelette by Walter F. Moudy, depicted the 2050 "Olympic War Games" between Russia and the United States. The games are fought to show the world the futility of war and thus deter further conflict. Each side has one hundred soldiers who fight in a large natural arena. The goal is for one side to wipe out the other; the few who survive the battle become heroes. The games are televised, complete with color commentary discussing tactics, soldiers' personal backgrounds, and slow-motion replays of their deaths.
* "[Bread and Circuses](/wiki/Bread_and_Circuses_(Star_Trek:_The_Original_Series) "Bread and Circuses (Star Trek: The Original Series)")" (1968) is an episode of the science fiction television series *[Star Trek](/wiki/Star_Trek:_The_Original_Series "Star Trek: The Original Series")* in which the crew visits a planet resembling the [Roman Empire](/wiki/Roman_Empire "Roman Empire"), but with 20th-century technology. The planet's "Empire TV" features regular [gladiatorial](/wiki/Gladiator "Gladiator") games, with the announcer urging viewers at home to vote for their favorites, stating, "This is your program. You pick the winner."
* *[The Year of the Sex Olympics](/wiki/The_Year_of_the_Sex_Olympics "The Year of the Sex Olympics")* (1968) is a [BBC](/wiki/BBC "BBC") television play in which a dissident in a dictatorship is forced onto a secluded island and taped for a reality show in order to keep the masses entertained.
* *[The Unsleeping Eye](/wiki/The_Unsleeping_Eye_(novel) "The Unsleeping Eye (novel)")* (1973), a novel by [D.G. Compton](/wiki/D.G._Compton "D.G. Compton") (also published as *The Continuous Katherine Mortenhoe*), is about a woman dying of cancer whose last days are recorded without her knowledge for a television show. It was later adapted as the 1980 movie *[Death Watch](/wiki/Death_Watch "Death Watch")*.
* "Ladies and Gentlemen, This Is Your Crisis" (1976) is a short story by science fiction author [Kate Wilhelm](/wiki/Kate_Wilhelm "Kate Wilhelm") about a television show in which contestants (including a B-list actress who is hoping to revitalize her career) attempt to make their way to a checkpoint after being dropped off in the Alaskan wilderness, while being filmed and broadcast around the clock through an entire weekend. The story focuses primarily on the show's effect on a couple whose domestic tensions and eventual reconciliation parallel the dangers faced by the contestants.
* The film *[Network](/wiki/Network_(1976_film) "Network (1976 film)")* (1976) includes a subplot in which network executives negotiate with an urban terrorist group for the production of a weekly series, each episode of which was to feature an act of terrorism. The climax of the film has the terrorist group being turned against the network's own unstable star, news commentator Howard Beale.
* *[The Running Man](/wiki/The_Running_Man_(King_novel) "The Running Man (King novel)")* (1982) is a book by [Stephen King](/wiki/Stephen_King "Stephen King") depicting a game show in which a contestant flees around the world from "hunters" trying to chase him down and kill him; it has been speculated that the book was inspired by "The Prize of Peril". The book was loosely adapted as a [1987 movie of the same name](/wiki/The_Running_Man_(1987_film) "The Running Man (1987 film)"). The movie removed most of the reality-TV element of the book: its competition now took place entirely within a large television studio, and more closely resembled an athletic competition (though a deadly one).
* The film *[20 Minutes into the Future](/wiki/20_Minutes_into_the_Future "20 Minutes into the Future")* (1985), and the spin-off television series *[Max Headroom](/wiki/Max_Headroom_(TV_series) "Max Headroom (TV series)")*, revolved around television mainly based on live, often candid, broadcasts. In one episode of *Max Headroom*, "Academy", the character Blank Reg fights for his life on a courtroom game show, with the audience deciding his fate.
* *[Vengeance on Varos](/wiki/Vengeance_on_Varos "Vengeance on Varos")* (1985) is a serial of the television show *[Doctor Who](/wiki/Doctor_Who "Doctor Who")* in which the population of a planet watches live television broadcasts of the torture and executions of those who oppose the government. The planet's political system is based on the leaders themselves facing disintegration if the population votes 'no' to their propositions.

## Pop culture references

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Reality_television&action=edit&section=42 "Edit section: Pop culture references")]

Some scripted and written works have used reality television as a plot device:

### Films

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Reality_television&action=edit&section=43 "Edit section: Films")]

* *[Real Life](/wiki/Real_Life_(1979_film) "Real Life (1979 film)")* (1979) is a comedic film about the creation of a show similar to *[An American Family](/wiki/An_American_Family "An American Family")* gone horribly wrong.
* *[Louis the 19th, King of the Airwaves](/wiki/Louis_the_19th,_King_of_the_Airwaves "Louis the 19th, King of the Airwaves")* (1994) is a [Québécois](/wiki/French-speaking_Quebecer "French-speaking Quebecer") film about a man who signs up to star in a 24-hour-a-day reality television show.
* *[The Truman Show](/wiki/The_Truman_Show "The Truman Show")* (1998) is a film about a man ([Jim Carrey](/wiki/Jim_Carrey "Jim Carrey")) who discovers that his entire life is being staged and filmed for a 24-hour-a-day reality television show.
* *[EDtv](/wiki/EDtv "EDtv")* (1999) was a remake of *[Louis the 19th, King of the Airwaves](/wiki/Louis_the_19th,_King_of_the_Airwaves "Louis the 19th, King of the Airwaves")*.
* *[Series 7: The Contenders](/wiki/Series_7:_The_Contenders "Series 7: The Contenders")* (2001) is a film about a reality show in which contestants have to kill each other to win.
* *[Halloween: Resurrection](/wiki/Halloween:_Resurrection "Halloween: Resurrection")* (2002) is a horror slasher film that takes place in a wired house full of surveillance cameras. Each "contestant" is recorded as they attempt to survive and solve the mystery of the murders.
* *[American Dreamz](/wiki/American_Dreamz "American Dreamz")* (2006) is a film set partially on an *American Idol*-like show.
* *[Slumdog Millionaire](/wiki/Slumdog_Millionaire "Slumdog Millionaire")* (2008) is a film in which a contestant on the Indian version of *[Who Wants to Be a Millionaire?](/wiki/Who_Wants_to_Be_a_Millionaire%3F "Who Wants to Be a Millionaire?")* is interrogated because he knows all the answers.

### Television

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Reality_television&action=edit&section=44 "Edit section: Television")]

* *[The Comeback](/wiki/The_Comeback_(TV_series) "The Comeback (TV series)")* (2005) satirizes the indignity of reality television by presenting itself as "raw footage" of a new reality show documenting the attempted comeback of has-been star [Valerie Cherish](/wiki/Valerie_Cherish "Valerie Cherish").
* The *[Doctor Who](/wiki/Doctor_Who "Doctor Who")* episode "[Bad Wolf](/wiki/Bad_Wolf "Bad Wolf")" (2005) is set in a space station, Satellite Five, where residents of Earth are randomly abducted and forced to participate in lethal incarnations of reality and quiz shows, such as *Big Brother* and *[The Weakest Link](/wiki/The_Weakest_Link_(British_game_show) "The Weakest Link (British game show)")* (the latter hosted by a robot modeled after the real show's then-host [Anne Robinson](/wiki/Anne_Robinson "Anne Robinson")).[[170]](#cite_note-170)[[171]](#cite_note-171)
* In the [season 5](/wiki/Nip/Tuck_season_5 "Nip/Tuck season 5") episode "Damien Sands" of American TV show *[Nip/Tuck](/wiki/Nip/Tuck "Nip/Tuck")* (2007), [Christian Troy](/wiki/Christian_Troy "Christian Troy"), jealous over [Sean McNamara](/wiki/Sean_McNamara_(Nip/Tuck) "Sean McNamara (Nip/Tuck)")'s newfound fame, convinces Sean to tape a reality show based on their careers as plastic surgeons, with disastrous results.
* *[Dead Set](/wiki/Dead_Set "Dead Set")* (2008) is a British television program featuring a zombie apocalypse affecting the *[Big Brother](/wiki/Big_Brother_(British_TV_series) "Big Brother (British TV series)")* house. Part of the film was shot during an actual eviction with host Davina McCall making a cameo appearance.
* *[Britain's Got the Pop Factor... and Possibly a New Celebrity Jesus Christ Soapstar Superstar Strictly on Ice](/wiki/Britain%27s_Got_the_Pop_Factor..._and_Possibly_a_New_Celebrity_Jesus_Christ_Soapstar_Superstar_Strictly_on_Ice "Britain's Got the Pop Factor... and Possibly a New Celebrity Jesus Christ Soapstar Superstar Strictly on Ice")* (2008) is a British comedy special that satirized the conventions of reality talent competitions (including, in particular, their reliance on emotional "sob stories" intended to promote empathy from viewers), [depicting](/wiki/Frame_story "Frame story") the series finale of the in-universe reality competition *Britain's Got the Pop Factor* (an amalgamation of *[Britain's Got Talent](/wiki/Britain%27s_Got_Talent "Britain's Got Talent")*, *[Pop Idol](/wiki/Pop_Idol "Pop Idol")*, and *[The X Factor](/wiki/The_X_Factor_(British_TV_series) "The X Factor (British TV series)"))*.[[172]](#cite_note-172)[[173]](#cite_note-173)
* *[Rock Rivals](/wiki/Rock_Rivals "Rock Rivals")* (2008) is a British television show about two judges on a televised singing contest whose marriage is falling apart.
* "[Fifteen Million Merits](/wiki/Fifteen_Million_Merits "Fifteen Million Merits")" (2011) is an episode in the first season of British television [anthology series](/wiki/Anthology_series "Anthology series") *[Black Mirror](/wiki/Black_Mirror "Black Mirror")*, set in a dystopian future in which appearing on reality television is the only way in which people can escape their miserable, jail-like conditions.
* *[Unreal](/wiki/Unreal_(TV_series) "Unreal (TV series)")* (2015) is an American television show that depicts the behind-the-scenes drama on a show similar to *The Bachelor*

### Literature

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Reality_television&action=edit&section=45 "Edit section: Literature")]

* *[Chart Throb](/wiki/Chart_Throb "Chart Throb")* (2006) is a comic novel by [Ben Elton](/wiki/Ben_Elton "Ben Elton") that parodies *[The X Factor](/wiki/The_X_Factor_(UK) "The X Factor (UK)")* and *[The Osbournes](/wiki/The_Osbournes "The Osbournes")*, among other reality shows.
* *[Dead Famous](/wiki/Dead_Famous_(novel) "Dead Famous (novel)")* (2001) is a comedic [whodunit](/wiki/Whodunit "Whodunit") novel, also by Ben Elton, in which a contestant is murdered while on a *Big Brother*-like show.
* *[Oryx and Crake](/wiki/Oryx_and_Crake "Oryx and Crake")* (2003), a speculative fiction novel by *[Margaret Atwood](/wiki/Margaret_Atwood "Margaret Atwood")*, occasionally makes mentions of the protagonist and his friend entertaining themselves by watching reality television shows of live executions, *[Noodie News](/wiki/Noodie_News "Noodie News")*, frog squashing, graphic surgery, and [child pornography](/wiki/Child_pornography "Child pornography").[[174]](#cite_note-174)[[175]](#cite_note-175)[[176]](#cite_note-176)
* *L.A. Candy* (2009) is a young adult novel series by [Lauren Conrad](/wiki/Lauren_Conrad "Lauren Conrad"), which is based on her experiences on *Laguna Beach: The Real Orange County* and *The Hills*.

## Other influences on popular culture

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Reality_television&action=edit&section=46 "Edit section: Other influences on popular culture")]

A number of scripted television comedy and satire shows have adopted the format of the documentary-type reality television show, in "[mockumentary](/wiki/Mockumentary "Mockumentary")" style. The first such show was the BBC series *[Operation Good Guys](/wiki/Operation_Good_Guys "Operation Good Guys")*, which premiered in 1997. Arguably the best-known and most influential such show is the BBC's *[The Office](/wiki/The_Office_(UK_TV_series) "The Office (UK TV series)")* (2001), which spawned numerous international remakes, including a [successful American version](/wiki/The_Office_(American_TV_series) "The Office (American TV series)"). Other examples include *[People Like Us](/wiki/People_Like_Us_(mockumentary) "People Like Us (mockumentary)")* (BBC UK, 1998), *[The Games](/wiki/The_Games_(Australian_TV_series) "The Games (Australian TV series)")* (ABC Australia, 1999), *[Trailer Park Boys](/wiki/Trailer_Park_Boys "Trailer Park Boys")* (2001), *[Reno 911!](/wiki/Reno_911! "Reno 911!")* (2003), *[The Naked Brothers Band](/wiki/The_Naked_Brothers_Band_(TV_series) "The Naked Brothers Band (TV series)")* (2006), *[Summer Heights High](/wiki/Summer_Heights_High "Summer Heights High")* (2007), *[Parks and Recreation](/wiki/Parks_and_Recreation "Parks and Recreation")* (2009), *[Modern Family](/wiki/Modern_Family "Modern Family")* (2009), *[Come Fly with Me](/wiki/Come_Fly_with_Me_(2010_TV_series) "Come Fly with Me (2010 TV series)")* (2010), *[Real Husbands of Hollywood](/wiki/Real_Husbands_of_Hollywood "Real Husbands of Hollywood")* (2013), *[Trial & Error](/wiki/Trial_%26_Error_(TV_series) "Trial & Error (TV series)")* (2017), *[Cut](https://ar.wikipedia.org/wiki/%D9%83%D8%AA_(%D9%85%D8%B3%D9%84%D8%B3%D9%84) "ar:كت (مسلسل)")* (2017) and *[Abbott Elementary](/wiki/Abbott_Elementary "Abbott Elementary")* (2021). The genre has even encompassed cartoons (*[Drawn Together](/wiki/Drawn_Together "Drawn Together")* (2004) and *[Total Drama](/wiki/Total_Drama "Total Drama")* (2007)) and a show about puppets (*[The Muppets](/wiki/The_Muppets_(TV_series) "The Muppets (TV series)")*, 2015).

Not all reality-television-style mockumentary series are comedic: the 2013 American series *[Siberia](/wiki/Siberia_(TV_series) "Siberia (TV series)")* has a science fiction-horror bent, while the 2014 Dutch series *[The First Years](/wiki/The_First_Years "The First Years")* is a drama.

The 2013–2015 American [sketch comedy](/wiki/Sketch_comedy "Sketch comedy") series *[Kroll Show](/wiki/Kroll_Show "Kroll Show")* set most of its sketches as excerpts from various fictional reality television shows, which one critic wrote "aren't far off from the lineups at E!, Bravo, and VH1", and parodied those shows' participants' "lack of self-awareness".[[177]](#cite_note-177) The show also satirized the often incestuous nature of reality television, in which some series lead to a cascade of spinoffs. *Kroll Show* executive producer John Levenstein said in an interview that reality TV "has so many tools for telling stories in terms of text and flashbacks and ways to show things to the audience that it's incredibly convenient for comedy and storytelling if you use the full reality show toolkit."[[178]](#cite_note-178)

Some feature films have been produced that use some of the conventions of reality television; such films are sometimes referred to as [reality films](/wiki/Reality_film "Reality film"), and sometimes simply as documentaries.[[179]](#cite_note-179) [Allen Funt](/wiki/Allen_Funt "Allen Funt")'s 1970 hidden camera movie *[What Do You Say to a Naked Lady?](/wiki/What_Do_You_Say_to_a_Naked_Lady%3F "What Do You Say to a Naked Lady?")* was based on his reality-television show *Candid Camera*. The series *[Jackass](/wiki/Jackass_(franchise) "Jackass (franchise)")* spawned five feature films, starting with *[Jackass: The Movie](/wiki/Jackass:_The_Movie "Jackass: The Movie")* in 2002. A similar Finnish show, *[The Dudesons](/wiki/The_Dudesons "The Dudesons")*, was adapted for the film *The Dudesons Movie*, and a similar British show, *[Dirty Sanchez](/wiki/Dirty_Sanchez_(TV_series) "Dirty Sanchez (TV series)")*, was adapted for *Dirty Sanchez: The Movie*, both in 2006. The producers of *The Real World* created *[The Real Cancun](/wiki/The_Real_Cancun "The Real Cancun")* in 2003. The Chinese reality show *[Keep Running](/wiki/Keep_Running_(TV_series) "Keep Running (TV series)")* was adapted for the 2015 film *[Running Man](/wiki/Running_Man_(2015_film) "Running Man (2015 film)")*.

The 2003 BBC film *[The Other Boleyn Girl](/wiki/The_Other_Boleyn_Girl_(2003_film) "The Other Boleyn Girl (2003 film)")* incorporated reality TV-style confessionals in which the two main characters talked directly to the camera.[[180]](#cite_note-180)

In 2007, broadcaster [Krishnan Guru-Murthy](/wiki/Krishnan_Guru-Murthy "Krishnan Guru-Murthy") stated that reality television is "a firm and embedded part of television's vocabulary, used in every genre from game-shows and drama to news and current affairs."[[181]](#cite_note-Has_reality_television_had_its_day?-181)

The [mumblecore](/wiki/Mumblecore "Mumblecore") film genre, which began in the mid-2000s, and uses video cameras and relies heavily on improvisation and non-professional actors, has been described as influenced in part by what one critic called "the spring-break psychodrama of MTV's *The Real World*". Mumblecore director [Joe Swanberg](/wiki/Joe_Swanberg "Joe Swanberg") has said, "As annoying as reality TV is, it's been really good for filmmakers because it got mainstream audiences used to watching shaky camerawork and different kinds of situations."[[182]](#cite_note-182)
